title: CPA Study Pack
note: FAR (F1–F4) + AUD (A1–A6) + REG (R1–R2), original & standards-derived. REG R3–R6 still pending.

questions.stem: In the auditor's report on a nonissuer's financial statements, which section is presented immediately after the Opinion section?
choices: Basis for Opinion
Key Audit Matters
Responsibilities of Management for the Financial Statements
Auditor's Responsibilities for the Audit of the Financial Statements
questions.explanation: The report leads with the Opinion section, immediately followed by the Basis for Opinion section, which references the audit standards, independence, and other ethical responsibilities. Key Audit Matters (when included) and both Responsibilities sections come later; the two Responsibilities sections do not appear until after Basis for Opinion.

questions.stem: The financial statements contain a departure from the applicable financial reporting framework whose effects are both material and pervasive. Which opinion is appropriate?
choices: Qualified opinion
Disclaimer of opinion
Unmodified opinion with an emphasis-of-matter paragraph
Adverse opinion
questions.explanation: A misstatement that is both material and pervasive requires an adverse opinion. A qualified (except-for) opinion applies when a misstatement is material but not pervasive. A disclaimer results from a scope limitation, not a known misstatement. An emphasis-of-matter paragraph never substitutes for modifying the opinion when the statements are materially misstated.

questions.stem: The auditor is unable to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence about a single account. The possible effects on the financial statements could be material but are not pervasive. The auditor should express:
choices: An adverse opinion
A disclaimer of opinion
A qualified opinion
An unmodified opinion with an other-matter paragraph
questions.explanation: A scope limitation whose possible effects are material but not pervasive results in a qualified opinion. A disclaimer is reserved for scope limitations that are both material and pervasive. An adverse opinion addresses known misstatements, not an inability to obtain evidence, and an other-matter paragraph does not cure a possible undetected misstatement.

questions.stem: An auditor engaged after year-end could not observe the client's beginning or ending physical inventory and cannot verify inventory quantities by alternative procedures. Inventory is the largest asset. The most appropriate report is:
choices: Qualified opinion for a scope limitation
Adverse opinion
Disclaimer of opinion
Unmodified opinion because inventory is an estimate
questions.explanation: Because the unverifiable amount is material and pervasive to the statements as a whole, the auditor disclaims an opinion. A qualified opinion would understate the significance of a pervasive scope limitation. An adverse opinion is used for pervasive misstatements, not an inability to obtain evidence, and the statements cannot be reported on cleanly merely because inventory involves estimation.

questions.stem: Which circumstance would ordinarily lead the auditor to add an emphasis-of-matter paragraph while still expressing an unmodified opinion?
choices: A substantial doubt about going concern that is adequately disclosed in the notes
An unjustified change from an acceptable accounting principle to an unacceptable one
A client-imposed restriction that prevented confirming receivables
Management's refusal to include a required disclosure
questions.explanation: An emphasis-of-matter paragraph highlights a matter that is appropriately presented or disclosed and fundamental to users' understanding; an adequately disclosed going-concern uncertainty is the classic example, and the opinion stays unmodified. An unacceptable accounting principle and an omitted required disclosure are misstatements that require modifying the opinion, and a client-imposed scope restriction leads to a qualified opinion or disclaimer rather than an emphasis-of-matter paragraph.

questions.stem: An other-matter paragraph, as distinguished from an emphasis-of-matter paragraph, is used to refer to a matter that is:
choices: Appropriately presented or disclosed in the financial statements and fundamental to users' understanding
A material misstatement that management has refused to correct
Not presented or disclosed in the financial statements but relevant to users' understanding of the audit, the auditor's responsibilities, or the report
A scope limitation imposed by management
questions.explanation: An other-matter paragraph addresses a matter not presented in the financial statements that is relevant to understanding the audit or report, such as noting a prior period audited by a predecessor or restricting the report's use. A matter that is disclosed and fundamental to understanding the statements belongs in an emphasis-of-matter paragraph, while uncorrected misstatements and scope limitations affect the opinion itself.

questions.stem: A client made a justified change in an accounting principle that is properly accounted for and adequately disclosed, and the auditor concurs with the change. The auditor should issue:
choices: A qualified opinion because consistency was not maintained
An unmodified opinion with an emphasis-of-matter paragraph describing the change
An unmodified opinion with no additional paragraph
An adverse opinion
questions.explanation: A justified, properly disclosed change in accounting principle affects comparability, so the auditor expresses an unmodified opinion and adds an emphasis-of-matter paragraph referring to the change. The opinion is not qualified or adverse because the change is acceptable and properly reported, and omitting the paragraph would fail to alert users to the consistency matter.

questions.stem: A group engagement partner decides not to assume responsibility for the work of a component auditor who audited a significant subsidiary. In the group auditor's report, the auditor should:
choices: Name the component auditor and take full responsibility for the subsidiary's amounts
Disclaim an opinion on the group financial statements
Add an emphasis-of-matter paragraph describing the division of responsibility
Refer to the component auditor, indicate the group opinion is based in part on that auditor's report, and disclose the magnitude of the portion audited
questions.explanation: When the group auditor does not assume responsibility, the report makes reference to the component auditor, states that the group opinion is based in part on that auditor's report, and discloses the magnitude of the portion audited. The component auditor is not named unless permission is given and that auditor's report is presented; making reference is not a basis for a disclaimer, and division of responsibility is conveyed in the opinion and basis sections rather than through an emphasis-of-matter paragraph.

questions.stem: Two months after the balance sheet date but before the report date, a major customer files for bankruptcy. The bankruptcy resulted from the customer's steadily deteriorating financial condition that already existed at the balance sheet date. The client should:
choices: Adjust the financial statements to reflect the collectibility of the receivable
Disclose the event in the notes without adjusting the statements
Take no action because the event occurred after year-end
Neither adjust nor disclose because the report is not yet issued
questions.explanation: Because the underlying condition existed at the balance sheet date, this is a recognized (Type I) subsequent event that requires adjusting the financial statements, such as increasing the allowance for doubtful accounts. Disclosure without adjustment describes a nonrecognized (Type II) event, which applies only when the condition arose after year-end; taking no action ignores information that clarifies conditions existing at the balance sheet date.

questions.stem: After the audit report date but before issuance, management discloses a significant subsequent event in the notes. The auditor decides to dual-date the report (for example, dating it February 5, 20X2 except for Note 12, as to which the date is February 20, 20X2). The effect of dual dating is that the auditor's responsibility for subsequent events is:
choices: Extended to all events occurring through February 20, 20X2
Limited to the specific event described in Note 12, with no extension of responsibility for other events after February 5
Eliminated for all subsequent events
Extended only if the event is a recognized (Type I) event
questions.explanation: Dual dating limits the auditor's responsibility for subsequent events to the single identified matter; responsibility for all other events ends at the original report date. Re-dating the entire report to the later date would instead extend responsibility to all subsequent events through that date. Dual dating neither eliminates responsibility nor depends on whether the event is Type I or Type II.

questions.stem: While auditing a nonissuer, the auditor reads the other information in the entity's annual report and identifies a material inconsistency. The financial statements are correct, but management refuses to revise the other information. The auditor should:
choices: Express a qualified opinion on the financial statements
Include a separate Other Information section in the report describing the uncorrected material misstatement of the other information
Disclaim an opinion on the financial statements
Issue an unmodified report and take no further action
questions.explanation: Because the misstatement is in the other information rather than the audited statements, the auditor does not modify the opinion on the financial statements; instead the report includes an Other Information section describing the uncorrected material misstatement. Qualifying or disclaiming the opinion would be inappropriate because the statements are fairly stated, and taking no action ignores the auditor's reporting responsibility for other information.

questions.stem: An auditor reports on financial statements prepared under the cash basis of accounting, a special purpose framework. The report should include:
choices: An emphasis-of-matter paragraph alerting users that the statements are prepared using a special purpose framework other than GAAP
An adverse opinion because the cash basis departs from GAAP
No additional communication, because the cash basis is acceptable
A disclaimer of opinion
questions.explanation: Reports on special purpose framework statements include an emphasis-of-matter paragraph describing the framework and alerting users that it is a basis other than GAAP (and, for regulatory or contractual bases, an other-matter paragraph restricting use). An adverse opinion is wrong because the statements are measured against the special purpose framework, not GAAP, and omitting the alert would leave users unaware of the basis of accounting.

questions.stem: In the standard audit report on the financial statements of an issuer under PCAOB standards, the auditor is required to communicate:
choices: Key audit matters (KAMs)
Critical audit matters (CAMs)
An emphasis-of-matter paragraph
A summary of uncorrected misstatements
questions.explanation: PCAOB standards require communication of critical audit matters (CAMs) in the reports of most issuers. Key audit matters (KAMs) are the analogous AICPA concept for nonissuers and are reported only when the auditor is engaged to do so. An emphasis-of-matter paragraph and a summary of uncorrected misstatements are not standard required elements of the issuer report.

questions.stem: A successor auditor is engaged to audit the current year of a nonissuer presenting comparative statements. The prior period was audited by a predecessor auditor whose report is not being reissued. In the current-year report, the successor should:
choices: Reaudit the prior period before issuing any report
Name the predecessor auditor and assume responsibility for the prior period
Add an other-matter paragraph stating that the prior period was audited by a predecessor, the type of opinion expressed, its date, and the reasons for any modification
Disclaim an opinion on the prior period
questions.explanation: The successor adds an other-matter paragraph disclosing that the prior period was audited by a predecessor auditor, the type of opinion issued, the date of that report, and, if the opinion was modified, the reasons, without naming the predecessor. Reauditing is unnecessary, the successor does not assume responsibility for the predecessor's work, and a disclaimer is unwarranted because the prior period was in fact audited.

questions.stem: Which body establishes the generally accepted auditing standards (issued as Statements on Auditing Standards) applicable to audits of nonissuers in the United States?
choices: The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)
The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
The Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB)
The Auditing Standards Board (ASB) of the AICPA
questions.explanation: The AICPA's Auditing Standards Board issues the Statements on Auditing Standards (codified as AU-C) that constitute GAAS for nonissuer audits. The PCAOB sets auditing standards for issuers, the SEC oversees securities markets and issuers rather than writing GAAS, and the FASB sets financial accounting (GAAP) standards, not auditing standards.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a precondition for an audit that the auditor must establish before accepting an engagement?
choices: Management acknowledges its responsibility for the financial statements and agrees to provide the auditor with unrestricted access to relevant information
Internal control over financial reporting is free of significant deficiencies
The auditor has completed preliminary analytical procedures
The engagement is expected to result in an unmodified opinion
questions.explanation: The preconditions for an audit require an acceptable financial reporting framework and management's acknowledgment of its responsibilities, including responsibility for the financial statements, for internal control, and for providing the auditor unrestricted access to information and personnel. A clean control environment and completed analytics occur during the audit, not as preconditions, and the auditor cannot presuppose the opinion before performing the work.

questions.stem: The primary purpose of an audit engagement letter is to:
choices: Express the auditor's opinion on the financial statements
Establish a written understanding of the terms of the engagement, including the responsibilities of management and the auditor
Provide reasonable assurance that fraud will be detected
Document the auditor's assessment of the risk of material misstatement
questions.explanation: An engagement letter documents the agreed terms, including the objective and scope of the audit, the responsibilities of management and the auditor, the applicable framework, and the expected form of the report, which reduces the risk of misunderstanding. It is not the vehicle for expressing the opinion, it does not promise fraud detection, and it is separate from the auditor's risk-assessment work.

questions.stem: A client asks the auditor to change the engagement from an audit to a review after the client refuses to permit confirmation of receivables. The auditor should:
choices: Agree, because the client has the right to change the type of engagement
Agree and issue a review report that references the scope limitation
Decline to change the engagement, because a scope restriction is not a reasonable basis for a change, and consider withdrawing
Agree only if the engagement fee is reduced accordingly
questions.explanation: A scope restriction, such as being barred from confirming receivables, is not a reasonable basis for changing an audit to a review; agreeing would let the client evade a likely disclaimer, so the auditor declines and considers withdrawing. Reasonable justification for a change generally involves a change in circumstances or a misunderstanding about the service, and the fee has no bearing on whether the change is justified.

questions.stem: The date of the auditor's report should be no earlier than the date on which the auditor has:
choices: Begun the fieldwork for the engagement
Delivered the signed report to the client
Reached the entity's balance sheet date
Obtained sufficient appropriate audit evidence to support the opinion, including evidence that management has taken responsibility for the financial statements
questions.explanation: The report date signals that the auditor considered the effects of events through that date, so it cannot precede the point at which sufficient appropriate audit evidence has been obtained, including evidence that management has taken responsibility for the statements. The start of fieldwork, delivery of the report, and the balance sheet date all precede that point and do not govern the report date.

questions.stem: In evaluating whether uncorrected misstatements are material, the auditor should consider:
choices: Both the magnitude and the nature of the misstatements, individually and in the aggregate
Only the total dollar amount of the misstatements relative to net income
Only those misstatements that management has refused to correct
Only misstatements that affect the balance sheet
questions.explanation: Materiality depends on both the size (magnitude) and the nature (qualitative characteristics) of misstatements, evaluated individually and in the aggregate. Looking only at total dollars, only at items management will not fix, or only at the balance sheet ignores qualitative factors and the combined effect across all of the statements.

questions.stem: Substantial doubt exists about a nonissuer's ability to continue as a going concern for a reasonable period of time, and management has adequately disclosed the matter. The auditor should express:
choices: A qualified opinion
An unmodified opinion with a separate section headed 'Substantial Doubt About the Entity's Ability to Continue as a Going Concern'
A disclaimer of opinion
An adverse opinion
questions.explanation: When substantial doubt about going concern exists and is adequately disclosed, the opinion remains unmodified and the report includes a separate section addressing the going-concern uncertainty. A qualified, adverse, or disclaimer of opinion is inappropriate because the statements are fairly presented and the matter is properly disclosed.

questions.stem: Substantial doubt exists about a nonissuer's ability to continue as a going concern, but the entity fails to make the disclosures required by the applicable financial reporting framework. The auditor should express:
choices: An unmodified opinion with a going-concern section
A disclaimer of opinion
A qualified or adverse opinion, because inadequate disclosure is a departure from the applicable financial reporting framework
An unmodified opinion with an other-matter paragraph
questions.explanation: If the entity omits the going-concern disclosures required by the framework, the omission is a departure from the applicable financial reporting framework, calling for a qualified or adverse opinion depending on materiality and pervasiveness. An unmodified opinion with any added paragraph, or a disclaimer, would not address a disclosure deficiency that constitutes a departure from the framework.

questions.stem: After releasing the audit report, the auditor learns of a fact that existed at the report date and would have affected the report. Management agrees to revise and reissue the financial statements. The auditor should:
choices: Take no further action because the report has already been issued
Withdraw the original report without performing additional procedures
Issue a disclaimer of opinion on the revised financial statements
Perform audit procedures on the revision and either dual-date the reissued report or date it as of the later date
questions.explanation: When a subsequently discovered fact that existed at the report date would have affected the report and management revises the statements, the auditor performs procedures on the revision and dates the reissued report using dual dating or the later date. Doing nothing, withdrawing without procedures, or automatically disclaiming ignores the auditor's responsibility to address facts existing at the report date.

questions.stem: An auditor is engaged to report on supplementary information that accompanies a nonissuer's audited financial statements. The auditor's reporting responsibility is to:
choices: Express a separate opinion on the supplementary information as though it were a complete set of financial statements
Provide no assurance, because the information is presented outside the basic financial statements
Express an opinion on whether the supplementary information is fairly stated, in all material respects, in relation to the financial statements as a whole
Disclaim an opinion on the financial statements because supplementary information is attached
questions.explanation: When engaged to report on supplementary information presented with audited statements, the auditor expresses an opinion on whether that information is fairly stated, in all material respects, in relation to the financial statements as a whole. It is not a stand-alone opinion as if the information were a complete set of statements, the auditor does provide assurance in relation to the statements, and attaching the information does not warrant disclaiming on the statements.

questions.stem: With respect to required supplementary information (such as GASB-required schedules) that accompanies the basic financial statements, the auditor should:
choices: Audit the required supplementary information and express an opinion on it
Ignore the required supplementary information entirely
Treat any omission of the required supplementary information as a departure from GAAP requiring a modified opinion on the basic financial statements
Apply limited procedures and include an other-matter paragraph, without expressing an opinion on the required supplementary information
questions.explanation: For required supplementary information, the auditor applies limited procedures, mainly inquiry of management and comparison for consistency, and adds an other-matter paragraph, without opining on the information. The auditor does not audit or opine on it, does not ignore it, and any omission is disclosed in the report rather than treated as a departure from GAAP in the basic financial statements.

questions.stem: Before accepting a new audit engagement, the successor auditor is expected to:
choices: Request the client's permission to communicate with the predecessor auditor and inquire about matters relevant to the decision to accept
Obtain the predecessor auditor's working papers as a condition of acceptance
Issue a report on the prior period audited by the predecessor
Notify regulators that a change of auditors has occurred
questions.explanation: Initiating communication with the predecessor (after obtaining the client's permission) is the successor's responsibility, and inquiries cover matters bearing on acceptance, such as disagreements and the reasons for the change. Access to the predecessor's working papers is customary but not a precondition, the successor does not report on the prior period the predecessor audited, and no regulator notification is required simply because the auditor changed.

questions.stem: After accepting an engagement, the firm discovers that a member of the engagement team is not independent. The firm should:
choices: Proceed and disclose the impairment in an other-matter paragraph
Evaluate the significance of the matter and, if independence cannot be satisfactorily resolved, withdraw from the engagement
Issue a qualified opinion because of the independence impairment
Ignore the matter if the affected individual is not the engagement partner
questions.explanation: Independence is a fundamental requirement; if it is impaired and cannot be resolved (for example, by removing the individual or eliminating the interest), the auditor cannot issue an opinion and should withdraw. An impairment cannot be cured by an added paragraph or a qualified opinion, and it matters regardless of whether the individual is the partner.

questions.stem: For an audit engagement that requires an engagement quality review, the reviewer must complete the review:
choices: Within 60 days after the report release date
Only if the client requests it
On or before the date of the auditor's report
After the audit documentation file has been assembled
questions.explanation: An engagement quality review must be completed before the report is released, so the review is finished on or before the report date. A post-release window would defeat the purpose of an objective evaluation before issuance, the review is not optional at the client's discretion, and it precedes final file assembly.

questions.stem: Overall responsibility for the quality of an audit engagement rests with:
choices: The staff assistants performing the fieldwork
The engagement quality reviewer
The firm's governing board
The engagement partner
questions.explanation: The engagement partner is responsible for the overall quality of the engagement, including its direction, supervision, and review. Staff perform assigned work under supervision, the engagement quality reviewer provides an objective evaluation but does not manage the engagement, and firm-wide governance sets the system of quality management rather than owning an individual engagement.

questions.stem: Under the AICPA's quality management standards, a firm designs its system of quality management using:
choices: A risk-based approach that establishes quality objectives, identifies quality risks, and designs responses
A fixed checklist of procedures that is identical for every firm
Only the findings of the firm's most recent peer review
The five components of the COSO internal control framework
questions.explanation: The quality management standards use a risk-based approach: the firm sets quality objectives, identifies and assesses quality risks, and designs responses tailored to its circumstances. A one-size-fits-all checklist contradicts the risk-based model, peer review results are not the design basis, and the firm's quality components are not the COSO internal control components.

questions.stem: Audit documentation should be sufficient to enable:
choices: The client to prepare its own financial statements
An experienced auditor with no previous connection to the audit to understand the procedures performed, the evidence obtained, and the conclusions reached
A new staff assistant to perform the audit without supervision
A regulator to re-perform the entire audit without the auditor's cooperation
questions.explanation: The standard for documentation is that an experienced auditor with no previous connection to the engagement could understand the nature, timing, and extent of procedures, the results and evidence obtained, and the significant findings and conclusions. It is not prepared to let the client draft statements, to substitute for supervision of new staff, or to enable an outsider to re-perform the whole audit unaided.

questions.stem: For an audit of a nonissuer, the auditor should assemble the final audit documentation file within:
choices: 30 days after the entity's balance sheet date
45 days after fieldwork begins
60 days following the report release date
One year after the report date
questions.explanation: The documentation completion date for a nonissuer audit is 60 days after the report release date, by which the auditor assembles the final file. The balance sheet date and the start of fieldwork are unrelated to file assembly, and one year is well beyond the required window.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the five components of internal control under the COSO framework?
choices: Control environment
Risk assessment
Detection risk
Monitoring activities
questions.explanation: The five COSO components are the control environment, risk assessment, control activities, information and communication, and monitoring activities. Detection risk is a component of the audit risk model used by the auditor, not a component of the entity's internal control.

questions.stem: In the COSO framework, which component sets the tone of the organization and provides the foundation for the other components of internal control?
choices: Control activities
Monitoring activities
Information and communication
The control environment
questions.explanation: The control environment establishes the tone at the top and the foundation on which the other four components rest, encompassing integrity, ethical values, and governance. Control activities, monitoring, and information and communication build upon that foundation rather than serving as it.

questions.stem: Which statement about internal control is correct?
choices: Internal control can provide only reasonable, not absolute, assurance because of inherent limitations such as human error, collusion, and management override
A well-designed system of internal control eliminates the risk of material misstatement
Internal control guarantees the reliability of financial reporting
Designing and maintaining internal control is the auditor's responsibility
questions.explanation: Because of inherent limitations, internal control can offer only reasonable assurance; human error, collusion among individuals, and management override can defeat even well-designed controls. Control therefore neither eliminates misstatement risk nor guarantees reliable reporting, and designing and maintaining it is management's responsibility, not the auditor's.

questions.stem: In a financial statement audit of a nonissuer, the auditor obtains an understanding of internal control primarily to:
choices: Express an opinion on the effectiveness of the entity's internal control
Identify and assess the risks of material misstatement and design the nature, timing, and extent of further audit procedures
Detect every instance of fraud within the entity
Prepare the entity's financial statements
questions.explanation: Understanding internal control lets the auditor identify where misstatements could occur and design responsive procedures. In a nonissuer financial statement audit the auditor is not opining on control effectiveness (that is an integrated audit or issuer requirement), cannot guarantee detection of all fraud, and does not prepare the client's statements.

questions.stem: How does the audit plan differ from the overall audit strategy?
choices: The audit plan is developed before the overall audit strategy
The audit strategy lists the specific procedures to be applied to each account
The audit plan is more detailed and describes the nature, timing, and extent of risk assessment procedures and further audit procedures
The audit plan is prepared only after the report date
questions.explanation: The overall strategy sets the scope, timing, and direction of the audit, and the more detailed audit plan flows from it, specifying the nature, timing, and extent of risk assessment and further audit procedures. The plan follows the strategy rather than preceding it, the strategy is higher level than an account-by-account procedure list, and planning occurs before and during the audit, not after the report.

questions.stem: Which statement about audit planning is correct?
choices: Planning is a discrete phase that must be fully completed before any audit evidence is gathered
The engagement partner is prohibited from involvement in planning to preserve objectivity
Planning is unnecessary for continuing (recurring) clients
Planning is a continual and iterative process that often begins shortly after the previous audit and continues throughout the current engagement
questions.explanation: Planning is continual and iterative rather than a single up-front step, and it may begin near the end of the prior audit and continue as the current engagement unfolds. The engagement partner and key team members are actively involved in planning, and recurring engagements still require planning that reflects current conditions.

questions.stem: When the external auditor uses the work of the entity's internal audit function, the external auditor:
choices: Has sole responsibility for the audit opinion, and that responsibility is not reduced by using the internal auditors' work
Shares responsibility for the opinion with the internal audit function
Must refer to the internal auditors in the auditor's report
Need not evaluate the internal auditors' competence or objectivity
questions.explanation: The external auditor remains solely responsible for the opinion; using internal auditors' work does not divide or reduce that responsibility. The internal auditors are not referenced in the report, and the external auditor must evaluate the function's competence, objectivity, and use of a systematic and disciplined approach before relying on its work.

questions.stem: An auditor engages an outside actuary as an auditor's specialist to help evaluate a pension obligation. The auditor should:
choices: Add an emphasis-of-matter paragraph disclosing the use of the specialist
Evaluate the specialist's competence, capabilities, and objectivity and remain solely responsible for the opinion, without referring to the specialist in an unmodified report
Attach the specialist's report to the auditor's report
Rely on the specialist's conclusions without any evaluation
questions.explanation: The auditor evaluates the specialist's competence, capabilities, and objectivity and the appropriateness of the work as evidence, while retaining sole responsibility for the opinion; a specialist is not referenced in an unmodified report. Using a specialist does not call for an added paragraph or attaching the specialist's report, and it never eliminates the auditor's obligation to evaluate the work.

questions.stem: The client used a valuation specialist it employed to measure a complex financial instrument, and the auditor intends to use that specialist's work as audit evidence. The auditor should:
choices: Conclude that information from a management's specialist cannot be used as audit evidence
Engage its own specialist to re-perform all of the specialist's work in every case
Evaluate the competence, capabilities, and objectivity of the management's specialist and the appropriateness of that specialist's work as audit evidence
Accept the specialist's work as automatically sufficient because financial statements are management's responsibility
questions.explanation: When using information from a management's specialist, the auditor evaluates that specialist's competence, capabilities, and objectivity, obtains an understanding of the work, and assesses whether it provides appropriate audit evidence. Such information can be used as evidence, re-performance of all the work is not always required, and management's overall responsibility does not make the specialist's work automatically sufficient for the auditor.

questions.stem: Performance materiality is best described as:
choices: The same amount as overall materiality for the financial statements as a whole
A threshold below which every misstatement is automatically considered material
An amount set by management for the auditor to use

An amount set below overall materiality to reduce, to an appropriately low level, the probability that the aggregate of uncorrected and undetected misstatements exceeds materiality for the statements as a whole
questions.explanation: Performance materiality is set below overall materiality to allow a margin for undetected and uncorrected misstatements that could aggregate to a material amount. It is therefore not equal to overall materiality, it is not a floor that makes small misstatements automatically material, and it is set by the auditor, not by management.

questions.stem: Tolerable misstatement is:
choices: The application of performance materiality to a particular sampling procedure or account balance
The maximum misstatement that management is willing to permit
Always equal to overall materiality for the financial statements
The amount of misstatement the auditor has actually detected during testing
questions.explanation: Tolerable misstatement applies performance materiality at the level of a specific account balance, class of transactions, or sampling procedure. It is an auditor concept rather than a management preference, it is generally below overall materiality, and it is a planning threshold rather than the misstatement actually found.

questions.stem: During the audit, the auditor becomes aware of information that would have led to a different materiality determination at the planning stage. The auditor should:
choices: Leave materiality unchanged because it was fixed during planning
Revise materiality (and, correspondingly, performance materiality) to reflect the new information
Revise materiality only after the report date
Revise performance materiality but never overall materiality
questions.explanation: Materiality is not fixed; if the auditor learns information that would have changed the initial determination, both overall materiality and performance materiality are revised, and the auditor considers whether the planned procedures remain appropriate. Deferring revision until after the report or refusing to revise overall materiality would leave the audit misaligned with the facts.

questions.stem: In the audit risk model, audit risk is a function of:
choices: Inherent risk only
The risk that the client's internal controls fail
The risk of material misstatement and detection risk
An amount set by the client
questions.explanation: Audit risk (the risk of expressing an inappropriate opinion when the statements are materially misstated) is a function of the risk of material misstatement and detection risk, where the risk of material misstatement combines inherent and control risk. It is not inherent risk alone, not simply the risk of control failure, and not a value chosen by the client.

questions.stem: For a given acceptable level of audit risk, if the auditor assesses the risk of material misstatement as higher than originally planned, the auditor should:
choices: Increase the acceptable level of detection risk
Increase control risk to compensate
Raise the materiality threshold
Decrease the acceptable level of detection risk, typically by performing more or more effective substantive procedures
questions.explanation: Detection risk varies inversely with the risk of material misstatement for a fixed audit risk, so a higher assessed risk of material misstatement requires lower detection risk, achieved through more, or more effective, substantive procedures. Increasing detection risk would raise audit risk, control risk is an assessment of the entity rather than a lever the auditor sets, and raising materiality does not offset a higher risk of material misstatement.

questions.stem: Which statement about detection risk is correct?
choices: Detection risk relates to the effectiveness of the auditor's procedures and can be changed by altering their nature, timing, and extent
Detection risk exists independently of the audit and cannot be affected by the auditor
Detection risk is a function of the client's internal control
Detection risk is the same as inherent risk
questions.explanation: Detection risk is the risk that the auditor's procedures fail to detect a material misstatement, and the auditor controls it by changing the nature, timing, and extent of testing. Inherent risk and control risk, by contrast, exist independently of the audit as characteristics of the entity, and detection risk is distinct from inherent risk.

questions.stem: The fraud triangle describes three conditions generally present when fraud occurs. Which of the following is one of those conditions?
choices: Adequate internal control
Incentive or pressure to commit fraud
A qualified audit opinion
A going-concern uncertainty
questions.explanation: The fraud triangle consists of incentive or pressure, opportunity, and attitude or rationalization. Adequate internal control reduces opportunity rather than being a fraud condition, and a qualified opinion or a going-concern uncertainty are outcomes or issues unrelated to the triangle's three elements.

questions.stem: Because management is in a unique position to override controls, on every audit the auditor is required to:
choices: Assess control risk at the maximum for all assertions
Confirm all account balances directly with third parties
Test the appropriateness of journal entries and adjustments, review accounting estimates for management bias, and evaluate the business rationale of significant unusual transactions
Withdraw from the engagement
questions.explanation: To address the risk of management override, the auditor must, in every audit, test journal entries and other adjustments, review estimates for bias, and evaluate the business rationale of significant unusual transactions. Maximizing control risk for everything, confirming every balance, or withdrawing are not the required responses to the override risk.

questions.stem: Professional skepticism requires the auditor to:
choices: Assume that management is dishonest until proven otherwise
Accept management's representations without corroboration when management appears trustworthy
Rely solely on the conclusions reached in the prior year's audit
Maintain a questioning mind and critically assess audit evidence, neither assuming management is dishonest nor assuming unquestioned honesty
questions.explanation: Professional skepticism is a questioning mindset and a critical assessment of evidence; the auditor presumes neither dishonesty nor unquestioned honesty. Assuming dishonesty, accepting representations without corroboration, or leaning entirely on prior-year conclusions each abandons the balanced, evidence-driven posture skepticism demands.

questions.stem: To obtain an understanding of the entity and its environment for assessing the risks of material misstatement, the auditor performs risk assessment procedures that include:
choices: Inquiries of management and others, analytical procedures, and observation and inspection
External confirmation of all receivables and payables
Recalculation of every material account balance
Reperformance of the client's monthly bank reconciliations
questions.explanation: Risk assessment procedures consist of inquiry, analytical procedures, and observation and inspection, and they build the understanding used to identify and assess risks. Confirmation, recalculation, and reperformance are substantive procedures aimed at detecting misstatement, not the procedures used to obtain the initial understanding.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an assertion about account balances at period end?
choices: Occurrence
Existence
Cutoff
Accuracy
questions.explanation: Existence, rights and obligations, completeness, and valuation and allocation are the assertions about account balances. Occurrence, cutoff, and accuracy are assertions about classes of transactions and events, not about ending balances.

questions.stem: To test the existence assertion for recorded accounts receivable, the auditor would:
choices: Trace shipping documents forward to the sales journal
Trace customer orders to recorded sales to verify all sales are recorded
Select recorded receivables and vouch them to shipping documents and customer confirmations
Recompute the allowance for doubtful accounts
questions.explanation: Existence is tested by starting with the recorded amount and vouching back to supporting evidence, confirming the receivable is real. Tracing from source documents forward tests completeness (that all items were recorded), and recomputing the allowance addresses valuation rather than existence.

questions.stem: The auditor is required to perform tests of controls when:
choices: In every audit, regardless of the audit approach
Never, because substantive procedures are always sufficient
Only when auditing an issuer
The auditor intends to rely on the operating effectiveness of controls, or substantive procedures alone cannot provide sufficient appropriate audit evidence
questions.explanation: Tests of controls are required when the auditor plans to rely on controls (assess control risk below maximum) or when substantive procedures alone cannot yield sufficient evidence, such as in highly automated processing. They are not mandatory in every audit, substantive testing is not always sufficient, and the requirement is not limited to issuers.

questions.stem: How does a test of controls differ from a substantive procedure?
choices: A test of controls evaluates the operating effectiveness of a control, while a substantive procedure is designed to detect material misstatements at the assertion level
A substantive procedure evaluates control effectiveness, while a test of controls detects misstatements
Both are designed solely to detect fraud
A substantive procedure only tests the design of controls
questions.explanation: Tests of controls address whether a control operated effectively, while substantive procedures (tests of details and substantive analytics) address whether the assertions are materially misstated. The reversed definition, a fraud-only purpose, and a design-only description are incorrect.

questions.stem: A dual-purpose test is one that:
choices: Audits two separate entities at the same time
Simultaneously serves as a test of controls and a substantive test of details on the same transaction
Is simply any procedure performed twice for reliability
Covers both the beginning and ending balance sheet dates
questions.explanation: A dual-purpose test examines a transaction both for whether the related control operated and for whether the amount is correctly recorded. It does not refer to auditing two entities, repeating a procedure, or spanning two balance sheet dates.

questions.stem: Regardless of the assessed risk of material misstatement or the degree of reliance on controls, the auditor must:
choices: Rely solely on tests of controls when controls are effective
Omit substantive procedures whenever control risk is assessed as low
Perform substantive procedures for each relevant assertion related to material classes of transactions, balances, and disclosures
Test 100% of all transactions
questions.explanation: Substantive procedures are required for all relevant assertions related to material items no matter how effective the controls are, so the auditor cannot rely on controls alone. Omitting substantive work when control risk is low, or testing every transaction, are both incorrect.

questions.stem: In the phrase 'sufficient appropriate audit evidence,' the two terms refer to:
choices: Both the quantity of evidence
Both the quality of evidence
Sufficiency is quality and appropriateness is quantity
Sufficiency is the quantity of evidence and appropriateness is its quality (relevance and reliability)
questions.explanation: Sufficiency measures how much evidence is needed, which is affected by risk and the quality of the evidence, while appropriateness measures relevance and reliability. The reversed pairing and the single-dimension descriptions are incorrect.

questions.stem: Which of the following ordinarily provides the most reliable audit evidence?
choices: A confirmation received directly by the auditor from an independent bank
A photocopy of a vendor invoice from the client's files
Oral responses to inquiries of the client's controller
A supporting schedule prepared by the client's accounting staff
questions.explanation: Evidence is more reliable when obtained directly by the auditor from a knowledgeable, independent source, so a directly received bank confirmation is the strongest option. Photocopies, oral inquiries, and client-prepared schedules are internally sourced or indirect and thus less reliable.

questions.stem: The use of negative accounts receivable confirmations is appropriate when:
choices: The assessed risk of material misstatement is high
The risk of material misstatement is low, there are many small homogeneous balances, few exceptions are expected, and recipients are likely to respond
The receivables consist of a few large balances
The auditor expects a high rate of exceptions
questions.explanation: Negative confirmations, which request a response only if the recipient disagrees, are appropriate only in low-risk, high-volume, homogeneous situations with few expected exceptions and where nonresponse can reasonably be taken as agreement. High risk, a few large balances, or expected exceptions call for positive confirmations instead.

questions.stem: Confirmation of accounts receivable is a presumptively required procedure. The auditor may omit it when:
choices: The client asks the auditor not to send confirmations for convenience
Sending confirmations would take longer than one week
Receivables are immaterial, confirmation would be ineffective, or the risk of material misstatement is low and other substantive procedures are sufficient
The auditor simply prefers to use analytical procedures
questions.explanation: Confirmation of receivables may be omitted only when the balance is immaterial, confirmation would be ineffective, or the combined assessed risk is low and other substantive evidence suffices. A client's mere preference, a time constraint, or the auditor's preference are not valid bases, and a client-imposed restriction would be a scope limitation.

questions.stem: Analytical procedures are required to be performed:
choices: Only as substantive tests of details
At every stage as the only source of evidence
Only when internal control is weak
During risk assessment (planning) and as part of the overall review near the end of the audit
questions.explanation: Analytical procedures are required at the risk assessment stage and again near the end of the audit as an overall review to corroborate the conclusions reached; their use as substantive procedures is optional. They are not the sole evidence source and are not limited to weak-control engagements.

questions.stem: A substantive analytical procedure is most effective when:
choices: The relationship among the data is plausible and predictable and the auditor develops a sufficiently precise expectation
The account balance is highly volatile and subject to management discretion
No predictable relationship exists among the data
The auditor is unable to develop an expectation
questions.explanation: Substantive analytics work when a plausible, predictable relationship lets the auditor build a precise expectation and investigate significant differences. Volatile, discretionary balances or the absence of a predictable relationship undermine the technique.

questions.stem: Which statement about inquiry as an audit procedure is correct?
choices: Inquiry is the most reliable form of audit evidence
Inquiry alone does not provide sufficient appropriate audit evidence and ordinarily must be corroborated by other procedures
A written response to an inquiry is conclusive evidence
Inquiry may substitute for confirmation of receivables
questions.explanation: Inquiry gathers information but by itself does not provide sufficient appropriate evidence about the absence of misstatement, so it is corroborated with other procedures. It is not the most reliable evidence, written responses are not conclusive, and inquiry does not replace confirmation.

questions.stem: With respect to related-party relationships and transactions, the auditor should:
choices: Presume that all related-party transactions are fraudulent
Disregard related-party transactions that occur in the normal course of business
Identify and assess the risks associated with related parties, remain alert for undisclosed ones, and evaluate their accounting and disclosure
Rely solely on management's list of related parties without further procedures
questions.explanation: The auditor assesses related-party risks, stays alert for relationships management has not disclosed, and evaluates the accounting and disclosure, giving special attention to significant transactions outside the normal course. Presuming fraud, ignoring normal-course transactions, or relying only on management's list are all inappropriate.

questions.stem: A user auditor wants to rely on the operating effectiveness of controls at a service organization that processes the client's transactions. The user auditor should obtain:
choices: A Type 1 report, because it tests operating effectiveness
A Type 1 report, which describes controls as of a point in time
No service auditor report, because the user auditor is not responsible for the service organization
A Type 2 report, which addresses both the design and operating effectiveness of controls over a period
questions.explanation: To rely on operating effectiveness, the user auditor needs a Type 2 report, which tests controls over a period; a Type 1 report only describes and opines on the design at a point in time. The user auditor remains responsible for the opinion and cannot forgo evidence about controls it intends to rely on.

questions.stem: When auditing an accounting estimate, the auditor may obtain evidence by:
choices: Testing management's process, developing an independent estimate, or reviewing subsequent events, while watching for indicators of management bias
Accepting the estimate without evaluation because estimates are inherently subjective
Requiring management to compute the estimate to an exact amount
Treating the presence of an estimate as a departure from the applicable framework
questions.explanation: Estimates are audited by evaluating management's process, independently developing an estimate, or examining events through the date of the report that confirm the estimate, all while assessing for bias. Estimates are not accepted without evaluation, are not expected to be exact, and their existence is not itself a departure from the framework.

questions.stem: Under the auditing standards on laws and regulations, the auditor's responsibility is:
choices: To detect every illegal act committed by the client
To obtain sufficient evidence of compliance with laws having a direct effect on material amounts, and to perform specified procedures for other laws that may have a material indirect effect
To report all violations directly to regulators
To disregard any law that does not directly affect the financial statements
questions.explanation: The auditor's responsibility is greater for laws with a direct effect on material financial statement amounts (obtain sufficient evidence of compliance) than for other laws (perform specified procedures to identify possible noncompliance). The auditor cannot be expected to detect every illegal act, is not a general reporter of all violations, and does not ignore indirect-effect laws entirely.

questions.stem: The auditor's primary means of corroborating management's information about litigation, claims, and assessments is:
choices: Reviewing news reports about the client
Inquiry of the client's internal audit function only
A letter of inquiry sent to the client's external legal counsel
Recalculating the estimated loss contingency
questions.explanation: A letter of inquiry to the entity's external legal counsel is the auditor's primary source of corroboration about litigation, and the lawyer's refusal to respond is a scope limitation. News reports, internal inquiry alone, and recomputation do not substitute for direct corroboration from counsel.

questions.stem: Sampling risk differs from nonsampling risk in that sampling risk is:
choices: The risk of applying an inappropriate audit procedure
The risk of misinterpreting the evidence obtained
The risk that the entity's controls fail to operate
The risk that a conclusion based on a sample differs from the conclusion that would result from examining the entire population
questions.explanation: Sampling risk arises because the sample may not represent the population, so the sample-based conclusion may differ from a 100% examination. Applying the wrong procedure or misinterpreting evidence are nonsampling risks, and control failure is a characteristic of the entity rather than a sampling concept.

questions.stem: Attribute sampling is used primarily to:
choices: Estimate the rate of deviation from a prescribed control, in support of tests of controls
Estimate the dollar amount of misstatement in an account balance
Confirm the existence of accounts receivable
Develop an independent estimate of a liability
questions.explanation: Attribute sampling estimates a deviation (occurrence) rate and is used in tests of controls. Estimating a dollar misstatement is the province of variables or monetary-unit sampling in substantive testing, and confirmation and independent estimation are unrelated to attribute sampling.

questions.stem: In a test of controls, which sampling risk relates to audit effectiveness?
choices: The risk of assessing control risk too high (underreliance)
The risk of assessing control risk too low (overreliance)
The risk of incorrect rejection
The risk of incorrect acceptance
questions.explanation: Assessing control risk too low (overreliance) causes the auditor to rely on a control that is not actually effective, impairing effectiveness; assessing control risk too high (underreliance) merely reduces efficiency. Incorrect acceptance and incorrect rejection are the corresponding risks in substantive sampling, not in tests of controls.

questions.stem: In substantive sampling for a test of details, the risk that most directly threatens audit effectiveness is:
choices: The risk of incorrect rejection
The risk of assessing control risk too high
The risk of incorrect acceptance, concluding a materially misstated balance is not misstated
Nonsampling risk arising from a clerical error
questions.explanation: The risk of incorrect acceptance impairs effectiveness because the auditor fails to detect a material misstatement; the risk of incorrect rejection impairs only efficiency by prompting unnecessary work. Assessing control risk too high is a tests-of-controls efficiency risk, and nonsampling risk is a separate category.

questions.stem: Holding other factors constant, the sample size for a substantive test of details increases when:
choices: Tolerable misstatement increases
The desired level of assurance (confidence) decreases
The variability of the population decreases
Tolerable misstatement decreases and expected misstatement increases
questions.explanation: A smaller tolerable misstatement and a larger expected misstatement both require a larger sample, as do higher population variability and a higher desired level of assurance. Larger tolerable misstatement, lower desired assurance, and lower variability all reduce the required sample size.

questions.stem: A characteristic advantage of audit data analytics (ADA) is that they:
choices: Eliminate the need for the auditor's professional judgment
Can be applied only during the risk assessment stage
Can analyze entire populations of transactions to identify outliers and anomalies for further investigation
Guarantee that all fraud will be detected
questions.explanation: Audit data analytics can examine 100% of a population to flag unusual items for follow-up, and they may be used in risk assessment, substantive procedures, and near completion. They do not remove the need for professional judgment, are not limited to one stage, and cannot guarantee detection of all fraud.

questions.stem: The primary substantive procedures for the existence of recorded accounts receivable are:
choices: Confirming receivables with customers and examining subsequent cash receipts
Searching for unrecorded liabilities
Observing the physical inventory count
Recomputing depreciation expense
questions.explanation: Existence of receivables is tested primarily by external confirmation with customers and by examining cash received after year-end. Unrecorded-liability searches address the completeness of payables, inventory observation addresses inventory, and depreciation recomputation addresses property and equipment.

questions.stem: To test sales cutoff, the auditor would:
choices: Confirm the total sales figure with customers
Examine shipping documents and sales invoices for a few days before and after year-end to verify sales were recorded in the correct period
Recompute the allowance for doubtful accounts
Observe the count of finished goods inventory
questions.explanation: Cutoff testing compares the shipment or transfer date with the recording date for transactions around year-end to confirm sales fall in the proper period. Confirming totals, recomputing the allowance, and observing counts do not address period cutoff.

questions.stem: Which procedure best addresses the completeness of accounts payable (the search for unrecorded liabilities)?
choices: Confirming recorded payables with vendors
Vouching recorded purchases to vendor invoices
Examining cash disbursements and unmatched receiving reports and vendor invoices after year-end
Observing the client's inventory count
questions.explanation: The search for unrecorded liabilities targets completeness by examining post-year-end disbursements and unrecorded invoices or receiving reports that relate to the period under audit. Confirming or vouching recorded payables tests what is already on the books rather than what may be missing.

questions.stem: A three-way match control over purchases consists of:
choices: Reconciling the monthly bank statement
Counting the physical inventory
Confirming accounts receivable with customers
Matching the purchase order, receiving report, and vendor invoice before recording and paying a liability
questions.explanation: A three-way match compares the purchase order, the receiving report, and the vendor invoice to confirm goods were ordered, received, and correctly billed before payment. Bank reconciliation, inventory counts, and receivable confirmations are unrelated procedures.

questions.stem: Which procedure requests account balances and information about loans and other arrangements directly from the entity's bank?
choices: The standard financial institution (bank) confirmation
An interbank transfer schedule
A cutoff bank statement
A proof of cash
questions.explanation: The standard financial institution confirmation requests deposit balances and details of the entity's loans and other arrangements directly from the bank. An interbank transfer schedule detects kiting, a cutoff bank statement corroborates reconciling items, and a proof of cash reconciles transactions over a period, none of which is the confirmation itself.

questions.stem: Which statement about kiting is correct?
choices: Kiting understates accounts receivable at year-end
Kiting overstates cash by recording a deposit from an interbank transfer before the offsetting disbursement clears, and it is detected using an interbank transfer schedule
Kiting is detected by confirming inventory with customers
Kiting relates to goods held on consignment
questions.explanation: Kiting inflates period-end cash by capturing the deposit side of an interbank transfer before the corresponding check clears the other account; an interbank transfer schedule reveals transfers spanning year-end. Kiting does not involve receivables, inventory confirmation, or consignment.

questions.stem: To verify the reconciling items (deposits in transit and outstanding checks) on the year-end bank reconciliation, the auditor would:
choices: Observe the physical inventory count
Confirm the loan balance with the lender
Obtain a cutoff bank statement covering the period after year-end and trace the reconciling items to it
Recompute depreciation expense
questions.explanation: A cutoff bank statement covering the days after year-end lets the auditor confirm that deposits in transit cleared promptly and outstanding checks cleared, corroborating the reconciliation. Inventory observation, debt confirmation, and depreciation recomputation address other accounts.

questions.stem: Which statement about the auditor's observation of the physical inventory count is correct?
choices: Inventory observation tests only the valuation assertion
The auditor performs the physical count on behalf of management
Observation is unnecessary whenever the entity maintains perpetual inventory records
The auditor observes the count to obtain evidence about existence and condition and performs independent test counts in both directions
questions.explanation: Attending the physical count provides evidence about the existence and condition of inventory, and the auditor makes independent test counts (floor-to-records for existence and records-to-floor for completeness). Observation is not primarily a valuation test, the count is management's responsibility, and perpetual records do not remove the observation requirement when inventory is material.

questions.stem: The valuation of inventory is tested by:
choices: Determining whether inventory is stated at the lower of cost and net realizable value and identifying obsolete or slow-moving items
Confirming inventory quantities with customers
Observing the physical count only
Recomputing interest expense
questions.explanation: Valuation testing confirms that inventory is not carried above the lower of cost and net realizable value and considers obsolescence and slow-moving goods. Customer confirmation, observation alone, and interest recomputation do not address inventory valuation.

questions.stem: With respect to goods held on consignment, which statement is correct?
choices: Consigned goods belong to the consignee and are included in the consignee's inventory
Goods held on consignment belong to the consignor and should be included in the consignor's inventory, not the consignee's
Consigned goods are expensed immediately by the consignor
Consignment arrangements have no effect on either party's inventory
questions.explanation: Title to consigned goods remains with the consignor, so the consignor reports them in inventory and the consignee excludes them; the auditor guards against double counting or omission. The other statements misstate ownership and accounting.

questions.stem: Existence and ownership of the entity's investments are tested primarily by:
choices: Observing the physical inventory count
Searching for unrecorded liabilities
Confirming securities held by a third-party custodian or inspecting securities on hand
Recomputing depreciation expense
questions.explanation: The auditor tests existence and ownership of investments by confirming holdings with the custodian or inspecting securities the entity holds. Inventory observation, unrecorded-liability searches, and depreciation recomputation address other accounts.

questions.stem: Which statement about the measurement of investment securities is correct?
choices: All investment securities are reported at historical cost
Held-to-maturity securities are reported at fair value through earnings
Trading securities are reported at amortized cost
Held-to-maturity debt securities are reported at amortized cost, while trading and available-for-sale securities are reported at fair value
questions.explanation: Classification drives measurement: held-to-maturity debt is carried at amortized cost, whereas trading and available-for-sale securities are measured at fair value (with unrealized gains and losses treated differently for each). The other options misstate these measurement bases.

questions.stem: Substantive procedures for property and equipment typically include:
choices: Vouching current-year additions to supporting invoices, recomputing depreciation, and inquiring about retirements
Confirming property and equipment balances with customers
Observing the physical inventory count
Confirming the balance with the entity's lender
questions.explanation: Property and equipment testing vouches additions to invoices, recomputes depreciation, and investigates disposals and unrecorded retirements. Customer confirmation, inventory observation, and lender confirmation address other cycles.

questions.stem: Effective control over payroll is achieved by:
choices: Having one person handle all payroll functions for efficiency
Separating the functions of authorizing employees (human resources), timekeeping, payroll preparation, and distribution of pay
Allowing supervisors to add employees to the payroll and approve their own time
Having the person who prepares payroll also distribute the paychecks
questions.explanation: Payroll control segregates human resources (authorizing employees and pay rates), timekeeping, payroll processing, and paycheck distribution so that no single person controls the whole process. Concentrating functions or letting the preparer distribute pay creates opportunities for fictitious-employee schemes.

questions.stem: Substantive procedures for long-term debt typically include:
choices: Observing the physical inventory count
Confirming the balance with the entity's customers
Confirming the loan balance and terms with the lender, recomputing interest, and reviewing compliance with debt covenants
Vouching recorded sales to shipping documents
questions.explanation: Debt is tested by confirming balances and terms with lenders, recomputing interest expense and accrued interest, and checking covenant compliance. Inventory observation, customer confirmation, and sales vouching relate to other cycles.

questions.stem: To audit shares outstanding, the auditor would:
choices: Confirm equity balances with the entity's customers
Observe the physical inventory count
Search for unrecorded liabilities
Confirm shares outstanding with the registrar or transfer agent, or examine stock records and board minutes for authorizations
questions.explanation: The auditor confirms outstanding shares with an independent registrar or transfer agent when one is used, or inspects the entity's stock records and board minutes for authorization of share issuances and dividends. Customer confirmation, inventory observation, and liability searches are unrelated.

questions.stem: A projected misstatement is best described as:
choices: The auditor's best estimate of the misstatement in a population, extrapolated from a sample
A misstatement about which there is no doubt
A misstatement arising only from management's judgment about an estimate
Any misstatement that management has already corrected
questions.explanation: A projected misstatement extrapolates the misstatement found in a sample to the whole population. A misstatement about which there is no doubt is a factual misstatement, a difference in a judgmental estimate is a judgmental misstatement, and corrected items are simply corrected.

questions.stem: During the audit, the auditor should:
choices: Ignore misstatements that fall below performance materiality
Accumulate misstatements identified during the audit other than those that are clearly trivial and communicate them to management on a timely basis, requesting correction
Record only the misstatements management agrees to correct
Wait until the report date to raise any misstatement
questions.explanation: The auditor accumulates misstatements that are more than clearly trivial and communicates them to the appropriate level of management on a timely basis, requesting correction. Ignoring items below performance materiality, recording only agreed corrections, or deferring all communication to the report date are improper.

questions.stem: With respect to uncorrected misstatements, the auditor should:
choices: Automatically issue an adverse opinion
Require management to correct only those above performance materiality
Communicate the uncorrected misstatements to those charged with governance and evaluate, individually and in the aggregate, whether they are material to the financial statements
Disregard the uncorrected misstatements if each one is individually immaterial
questions.explanation: Uncorrected misstatements are communicated to those charged with governance and evaluated both individually and in the aggregate (and qualitatively) against materiality to judge the effect on the opinion. An automatic adverse opinion, a performance-materiality correction rule, or ignoring the aggregate effect are all incorrect.

questions.stem: Which statement about management's written representations is correct?
choices: Written representations can replace substantive audit procedures
Written representations guarantee that the financial statements are free of misstatement
Written representations are optional and obtained only at the client's request
Written representations are necessary audit evidence but are not a substitute for other evidence the auditor could reasonably expect to obtain
questions.explanation: Management's written representations corroborate other evidence and confirm management's responsibilities, but they do not substitute for procedures the auditor would otherwise perform and do not guarantee the absence of misstatement; they are required, not optional.

questions.stem: The management representation letter should be:
choices: Dated as of the date of the auditor's report and obtained from members of management with overall responsibility for the financial statements
Dated as of the balance sheet date and signed by any available employee
Dated after the auditor's report is issued
Signed only by the entity's external attorney
questions.explanation: The representation letter is dated as of the auditor's report date and is signed by management with overall responsibility for and knowledge of the financial statements, typically the chief executive and chief financial officers. A balance sheet date, a post-issuance date, an arbitrary employee, or the attorney are all incorrect.

questions.stem: Management's refusal to provide requested written representations:
choices: Has no effect on the auditor's report
Constitutes a scope limitation that ordinarily leads to a disclaimer of opinion or withdrawal from the engagement
Requires only an emphasis-of-matter paragraph
Results in a qualified opinion for a departure from the applicable framework
questions.explanation: A refusal to furnish requested written representations is a scope limitation so significant that the auditor ordinarily disclaims an opinion or withdraws, and it also casts doubt on the reliability of other representations. It is not ignorable, is not resolved by an added paragraph, and is not a framework departure.

questions.stem: Required communications with those charged with governance include:
choices: The specific fees the firm charges the client's competitors
A guarantee that no fraud exists within the entity
The auditor's responsibilities under the standards, the planned scope and timing of the audit, and significant findings from the audit
The individual salaries of the members of the audit team
questions.explanation: Required communications include the auditor's responsibilities, the planned scope and timing, and significant audit findings, such as difficulties encountered, disagreements with management, and significant matters. Competitor fees, fraud guarantees, and team salaries are not required communications.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes a material weakness from a significant deficiency?
choices: A significant deficiency creates a reasonable possibility that a material misstatement will not be prevented or detected, while a material weakness is less severe
Both require an adverse opinion on the financial statements
A significant deficiency is always more severe than a material weakness

A material weakness is a deficiency, or combination of deficiencies, such that there is a reasonable possibility that a material misstatement will not be prevented or timely detected, while a significant deficiency is less severe but important enough to merit the attention of those charged with governance
questions.explanation: A material weakness carries a reasonable possibility that a material misstatement would not be prevented or detected on a timely basis, while a significant deficiency is less severe but still warrants governance attention. The reversed severity, an automatic adverse opinion on the statements, and the claim that significant deficiencies are more severe are all incorrect.

questions.stem: When the auditor identifies significant deficiencies and material weaknesses in internal control, the auditor should:
choices: Issue a written statement that no material weaknesses were identified
Communicate the deficiencies only orally to management
Communicate significant deficiencies and material weaknesses in writing to management and those charged with governance
Report the deficiencies directly to regulators
questions.explanation: Significant deficiencies and material weaknesses identified during the audit are communicated in writing to management and those charged with governance; the auditor should not issue a written communication stating that no material weaknesses were identified in a nonissuer audit. Oral-only communication or reporting to regulators is not the required approach.

questions.stem: An integrated audit is best described as:
choices: An audit that combines an audit of the financial statements with an audit of the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting, performed for issuers under PCAOB standards
An audit performed jointly by two independent firms
An audit of internal control only, with no audit of the financial statements
An engagement that merges the entity's tax and financial reporting
questions.explanation: An integrated audit pairs the financial statement audit with an audit of the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting and is required for issuers under PCAOB standards. It is not a joint two-firm audit, not an internal-control-only engagement, and not a merger of tax and financial reporting.

questions.stem: In an integrated audit, the auditor's opinion on the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting is expressed:
choices: For the entire period covered by the financial statements
As of the end of the entity's fiscal year (a point in time)
As of the report release date
For a rolling twelve months after year-end
questions.explanation: The ICFR opinion is expressed as of the fiscal year-end, a point in time, while the financial statement opinion covers the period then ended. It is not a period opinion, not as of the report release date, and not a forward rolling window.

questions.stem: To select controls to test in an integrated audit, the auditor uses:
choices: A requirement to test every control in the entity
A bottom-up approach starting with individual transactions
A top-down, risk-based approach that begins at the financial statement level with entity-level controls, then focuses on significant accounts, disclosures, and relevant assertions
Reliance solely on management's own assessment of internal control
questions.explanation: The auditor applies a top-down, risk-based approach, starting with entity-level controls and narrowing to significant accounts, disclosures, and their relevant assertions. Testing every control, working bottom-up from transactions, or relying solely on management's assessment are incorrect.

questions.stem: When testing the controls selected in an integrated audit, the auditor should:
choices: Test only the design of the controls
