Test only the operating effectiveness of the controls
Rely on inquiry of management alone
Test both the design and the operating effectiveness of the selected controls
questions.explanation: The auditor tests both whether the selected controls are designed effectively and whether they operated effectively during the period. Inquiry alone is insufficient, and testing design or operating effectiveness in isolation would be incomplete.

questions.stem: If a material weakness in internal control over financial reporting exists at year-end, the auditor should:
choices: Express an adverse opinion on the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting
Express a qualified opinion on internal control
Express an unqualified opinion on internal control with an explanatory paragraph
Disclaim an opinion on internal control
questions.explanation: A material weakness existing at year-end results in an adverse opinion on the effectiveness of ICFR. A qualified or unqualified opinion would understate the deficiency, and a disclaimer is reserved for scope limitations rather than an identified material weakness.

questions.stem: Can an auditor issue an unmodified opinion on the financial statements and an adverse opinion on internal control over financial reporting in the same integrated audit?
choices: No, an adverse ICFR opinion requires an adverse opinion on the financial statements
Yes, the financial statements may be fairly stated even though a material weakness produces an adverse ICFR opinion
No, the two opinions must always agree
Yes, but only in a nonissuer engagement
questions.explanation: The financial statement and ICFR opinions are separate; a material weakness yields an adverse ICFR opinion, yet the statements may still be fairly stated because the auditor's substantive procedures detected and corrected any misstatement. The opinions need not agree, and integrated audits concern issuers, not nonissuers.

questions.stem: If the auditor is unable to obtain sufficient evidence to support an opinion on internal control over financial reporting, the auditor should:
choices: Issue an adverse opinion on internal control
Issue an unqualified opinion on internal control with an explanatory paragraph
Disclaim an opinion on internal control or withdraw from the internal control engagement
Ignore the limitation and rely on management's assessment
questions.explanation: A scope limitation on the ICFR audit leads the auditor to disclaim an opinion on internal control (or withdraw), paralleling the disclaimer used for a pervasive scope limitation in a financial statement audit. An adverse opinion addresses an identified material weakness, not an inability to obtain evidence, and the limitation cannot be ignored.

questions.stem: Which statement about the three types of attestation engagements is correct?
choices: All three provide the same level of reasonable assurance
A review provides more assurance than an examination
An agreed-upon procedures engagement provides the highest level of assurance
An examination provides reasonable assurance (an opinion), a review provides limited assurance (a conclusion), and an agreed-upon procedures engagement provides no assurance (findings only)
questions.explanation: An examination gives reasonable assurance through an opinion, a review gives limited (negative) assurance through a conclusion, and an agreed-upon procedures engagement provides no assurance, reporting only findings. The other statements misrank the assurance levels.

questions.stem: In an attestation examination engagement, the practitioner:
choices: Expresses an opinion providing reasonable assurance about whether the subject matter (or an assertion about it) is in accordance with the criteria
Provides only negative (limited) assurance
Provides no assurance and reports only findings
Performs a compilation with no assurance
questions.explanation: An examination expresses an opinion and provides reasonable assurance about whether the subject matter conforms to the criteria (or whether the assertion is fairly stated). Negative assurance describes a review, no assurance describes agreed-upon procedures, and a compilation is not an attestation examination.

questions.stem: In an attestation review engagement, the practitioner expresses:
choices: An opinion providing reasonable assurance
A conclusion providing limited (negative) assurance, stating that nothing came to the practitioner's attention indicating the subject matter is not in accordance with the criteria
No assurance of any kind
An adverse opinion
questions.explanation: A review provides limited assurance through a conclusion worded in the negative, indicating that nothing came to the practitioner's attention suggesting noncompliance with the criteria. Reasonable assurance describes an examination, no assurance describes agreed-upon procedures, and an adverse opinion is not the review form.

questions.stem: In an agreed-upon procedures engagement, the practitioner:
choices: Expresses an opinion on the subject matter as a whole
Determines which procedures are sufficient for the engagement
Performs specific procedures agreed upon by the specified parties and reports findings without expressing an opinion or conclusion, with use of the report restricted to those parties
Issues a report for general distribution
questions.explanation: In an agreed-upon procedures engagement the specified parties agree to and take responsibility for the sufficiency of the procedures, the practitioner reports factual findings without providing assurance, and the report's use is restricted to those parties. The practitioner does not express an opinion, does not decide sufficiency, and the report is not for general use.

questions.stem: How does a financial forecast differ from a financial projection?
choices: A forecast is based on hypothetical assumptions, while a projection is based on expected conditions
Both are based on hypothetical assumptions
Both must be issued for general use
A forecast is based on expected conditions and expected courses of action, while a projection is based on one or more hypothetical assumptions
questions.explanation: A forecast reflects the conditions the entity expects and the actions it expects to take, whereas a projection is built on one or more hypothetical, what-if assumptions. The first option reverses the definitions, and the others misstate the assumption basis and use.

questions.stem: With respect to the permitted use of prospective financial statements:
choices: The use of a financial projection is restricted to the specified parties, whereas a financial forecast may be issued for general use
Both a forecast and a projection may be issued for general use
Both a forecast and a projection are restricted to specified parties
A projection may be for general use while a forecast must be restricted
questions.explanation: Because a projection rests on hypothetical assumptions, its use is limited to the specified parties who understand those assumptions, while a forecast may be presented for general use. The other options misstate which presentation may be generally used.

questions.stem: When reporting on prospective financial statements, the practitioner:
choices: Guarantees that the prospective results will be achieved
Does not provide assurance that the prospective results will be achieved and includes a caveat that actual results may differ
Audits the entity's future results
Insures the accuracy of the forecast
questions.explanation: The practitioner does not vouch for the achievability of prospective results; the report includes a statement that there will usually be differences between forecasted or projected and actual results. The practitioner cannot guarantee, audit the future, or insure the outcome.

questions.stem: A SOC 1 report on controls at a service organization is:
choices: A general-use report on the service organization's own financial statements
A report that the user auditor prepares about the service organization
A service auditor's report on controls relevant to user entities' internal control over financial reporting, with use restricted to management, user entities, and their auditors
A report that eliminates the user auditor's responsibility for the opinion
questions.explanation: A SOC 1 report is issued by the service auditor on controls at the service organization that are relevant to user entities' financial reporting, and its use is restricted to the service organization's management, user entities, and their auditors. It is not a general-use financial statement report, not prepared by the user auditor, and does not relieve the user auditor of responsibility for the opinion.

questions.stem: In an examination of compliance (a compliance audit or attestation examination), the practitioner:
choices: Provides no assurance about compliance
Reports only a list of exceptions
Audits the entity's financial statements instead of compliance
Expresses an opinion on whether the entity complied, in all material respects, with the specified compliance requirements
questions.explanation: A compliance examination results in an opinion on whether the entity complied, in all material respects, with the applicable compliance requirements. Providing no assurance describes agreed-upon procedures, a findings-only list is not an examination, and a compliance engagement addresses compliance rather than the financial statements.

questions.stem: Generally Accepted Government Auditing Standards (GAGAS) are issued by:
choices: The U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO), and are commonly called the Yellow Book
The American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)
The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)
The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
questions.explanation: GAGAS are issued by the GAO and are commonly known as the Yellow Book; they build on GAAS with additional requirements. The AICPA issues GAAS for nonissuers, the PCAOB sets standards for issuers, and the SEC oversees securities markets.

questions.stem: In a financial audit performed under GAGAS, in addition to the opinion on the financial statements, the auditor:
choices: Issues only the financial statement opinion, identical to a nonissuer audit under GAAS
Issues a written report on internal control over financial reporting and on compliance with laws, regulations, contracts, and grant agreements
Issues an opinion on the effectiveness of internal control, as in an issuer's integrated audit
Issues no report on internal control
questions.explanation: GAGAS requires a written report on internal control over financial reporting and on compliance with laws, regulations, contracts, and grant agreements, in addition to the opinion on the statements. It is more than a plain GAAS report, but it does not opine on ICFR effectiveness the way an issuer's integrated audit does.

questions.stem: Under GAGAS, with respect to independence, auditors should:
choices: Not be concerned with independence for government engagements
Assume that nonaudit services never affect independence
Apply a conceptual framework to independence and recognize that providing certain nonaudit services, such as assuming management responsibilities, can impair independence
Apply independence requirements only when auditing issuers
questions.explanation: GAGAS requires auditors to apply a conceptual framework to evaluate threats to independence and to recognize that nonaudit services, particularly assuming management responsibilities, can impair independence. Independence is required, nonaudit services can affect it, and the requirement is not limited to issuers.

questions.stem: Auditors performing engagements under GAGAS are required to:
choices: Have no continuing education requirements
Be licensed as attorneys
Work only for government agencies
Meet continuing professional education requirements established under GAGAS
questions.explanation: GAGAS establishes continuing professional education requirements to help ensure auditors maintain competence for government engagements. There is no attorney-licensing requirement, auditors need not be government employees, and the CPE requirement does apply.

questions.stem: A single audit under the Uniform Guidance is generally required for:
choices: A non-federal entity that expends federal awards above the threshold established by the Uniform Guidance during its fiscal year
Every entity that receives any federal funding, regardless of the amount
Only publicly traded companies
Only federal agencies themselves
questions.explanation: A single audit (or a program-specific audit) is required when a non-federal entity expends federal awards above the threshold set by the Uniform Guidance in a fiscal year. It is not triggered by any federal funding regardless of amount, is not limited to public companies, and does not apply to federal agencies themselves.

questions.stem: A single audit encompasses:
choices: Only an audit of the entity's financial statements
Both an audit of the entity's financial statements and an audit of its compliance with requirements applicable to its major federal programs
Only a tax examination
An audit of every federal program regardless of size
questions.explanation: A single audit combines an audit of the financial statements with an audit of compliance for the entity's major federal programs. It is more than a financial statement audit, it is not a tax examination, and not every program is audited as a major program.

questions.stem: Under the Uniform Guidance, major federal programs are identified:
choices: By auditing all of the entity's federal programs
By selecting programs alphabetically
Using a risk-based approach that considers program size (Type A versus Type B programs) and prior audit findings, rather than auditing every program
By selecting only the smallest programs
questions.explanation: Major programs are determined with a risk-based approach that first distinguishes larger Type A programs from smaller Type B programs and factors in prior audit findings and risk, so not every program is audited as major. Auditing all programs, alphabetical selection, or focusing on the smallest programs are incorrect.

questions.stem: In a single audit, with respect to federal programs the auditor issues:
choices: Only an opinion on the financial statements
No report on compliance
An opinion on compliance for every federal program
An opinion on compliance for each major program and a report on internal control over compliance
questions.explanation: The single audit produces an opinion on compliance for each major program together with a report on internal control over compliance, in addition to the financial statement reporting. The auditor does report on compliance, and compliance opinions relate to major programs rather than every program.

questions.stem: In a single audit, the Schedule of Expenditures of Federal Awards (SEFA) is:
choices: A schedule the federal government prepares on behalf of the entity
A federal tax return filed for the awards
A schedule of the entity's federal award expenditures that the auditor reports on in relation to the financial statements as a whole
A list of the entity's employees
questions.explanation: The SEFA presents the entity's expenditures of federal awards and is reported on by the auditor in relation to the financial statements as a whole. It is prepared by the auditee (not the government), is not a tax return, and is not an employee listing.

questions.stem: The Statements on Standards for Accounting and Review Services (SSARS) are issued by:
choices: The AICPA's Accounting and Review Services Committee (ARSC), and they apply to preparation, compilation, and review engagements for nonissuers
The PCAOB, and they apply to issuers
The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
The U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO)
questions.explanation: SSARS are issued by the AICPA's Accounting and Review Services Committee and govern preparation, compilation, and review engagements performed for nonissuers. The PCAOB sets issuer audit standards, the SEC oversees securities markets, and the GAO issues government auditing standards.

questions.stem: In a preparation engagement under SSARS (AR-C 70), the accountant:
choices: Provides limited assurance on the financial statements
Prepares the financial statements but provides no assurance, issues no report, and includes a statement on each page that no assurance is provided (or a disclaimer); independence is not required
Must issue a compilation report
Must be independent of the client
questions.explanation: A preparation engagement produces financial statements with no assurance and no report; each page states that no assurance is provided (or a disclaimer accompanies the statements), and independence is not required. It is a nonattest service, so limited assurance, a compilation report, and an independence requirement do not apply.

questions.stem: In a compilation engagement under SSARS (AR-C 80), the accountant:
choices: Provides limited assurance on the financial statements
Provides reasonable assurance on the financial statements
Presents financial statements without providing any assurance, issues a compilation report, and is not required to verify or make inquiries about the information
Must perform inquiry and analytical procedures like a review
questions.explanation: A compilation results in financial statements accompanied by a compilation report but provides no assurance, and the accountant is not required to verify or make inquiries about the underlying information. Limited or reasonable assurance and review-level procedures are not part of a compilation.

questions.stem: When the accountant is not independent of the client, in a compilation engagement the accountant:
choices: Cannot perform the engagement
Must withdraw from the engagement
Must disclose the specific reasons for the lack of independence
May still perform the compilation but must disclose the lack of independence in the compilation report
questions.explanation: In a compilation the accountant need not be independent but must disclose a lack of independence in the report; disclosing the reason(s) for the lack of independence is permitted but not required. The accountant is not barred from performing the engagement and need not withdraw.

questions.stem: A review engagement under SSARS (AR-C 90):
choices: Provides limited assurance, obtained primarily through inquiry of management and analytical procedures
Provides reasonable assurance obtained through corroborating audit procedures
Provides no assurance
Requires tests of controls
questions.explanation: A review provides limited assurance obtained mainly through inquiry and analytical procedures. Reasonable assurance and corroborating procedures describe an audit, no assurance describes a compilation or preparation, and tests of controls are not part of a review.

questions.stem: With respect to independence in a review engagement under SSARS:
choices: Independence is not required for a review
The accountant must be independent; if not independent, the accountant cannot issue a review report
The accountant may perform a review while merely disclosing a lack of independence
Independence is required only when the client is an issuer
questions.explanation: Independence is required for a review because it is an attest engagement; an accountant who is not independent cannot issue a review report. This differs from a compilation, where a lack of independence may simply be disclosed.

questions.stem: The procedures performed in a SSARS review consist primarily of:
choices: Confirmation of all significant receivables
Observation of the physical inventory count
Inquiry of management and analytical procedures, which are less extensive than the procedures in an audit
Tests of the operating effectiveness of controls
questions.explanation: A review is performed mainly through inquiry and analytical procedures, providing limited assurance with a lower level of effort than an audit. Confirmation, inventory observation, and tests of controls are audit procedures not required in a review.

questions.stem: The conclusion in a SSARS review report expresses:
choices: An opinion that the financial statements are fairly presented in all material respects
A statement that no assurance is provided
An adverse opinion on the financial statements

A conclusion providing limited assurance, stating that the accountant is not aware of any material modifications that should be made for the statements to be in accordance with the applicable framework
questions.explanation: A review report expresses limited assurance through a conclusion that the accountant is not aware of any material modifications needed for the statements to conform with the framework. An opinion describes an audit, a no-assurance statement describes a preparation or compilation, and an adverse opinion is not the review form.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly ranks the assurance provided by accounting and audit services?
choices: A preparation engagement provides no assurance and no report, a compilation provides no assurance with a report, a review provides limited assurance, and an audit provides reasonable assurance
All four services provide the same level of assurance
A compilation provides more assurance than a review
A review provides more assurance than an audit
questions.explanation: The services form a ladder: preparation (no assurance, no report), compilation (no assurance, with a report), review (limited assurance), and audit (reasonable assurance). The other options misrank these levels.

questions.stem: In a SSARS review engagement, the accountant is required to:
choices: Confirm all significant account balances with third parties
Obtain a management representation letter
Test the entity's internal controls
Express an opinion on the financial statements
questions.explanation: A review requires the accountant to obtain a management representation letter, but it does not require confirmation, control testing, or an opinion; those belong to an audit. A review provides only limited assurance through inquiry and analytics.

questions.stem: A review of interim financial information under the auditing standards (AU-C 930):
choices: Provides reasonable assurance (an opinion) on the interim information
Is an audit of the interim financial statements

Provides limited assurance on the interim financial information of an entity whose annual financial statements are audited, and the accountant should have an understanding of the entity's internal control
Requires no knowledge of the entity's internal control
questions.explanation: An interim review provides limited assurance on interim information for an entity whose annual statements are audited, and it presumes the accountant has an understanding of the entity's internal control. It is not an audit and does not provide an opinion, and knowledge of internal control is expected.

questions.stem: When a client asks the accountant to change an audit engagement to a review or compilation, the accountant should:
choices: Always refuse to change the engagement
Automatically agree to the change without further consideration
Issue the originally agreed report regardless of the request
Consider the reason for the request and whether it is reasonable before agreeing to change to a lower level of service
questions.explanation: The accountant evaluates the reason for the change and whether it is reasonable; a change in client needs may be reasonable, whereas a request made to avoid a scope limitation or a modified opinion is not. Refusing outright, automatically agreeing, or issuing the original report are incorrect.

questions.stem: How does a preparation engagement differ from a compilation engagement?
choices: A preparation engagement produces financial statements with no report attached, whereas a compilation engagement results in the issuance of a compilation report
Both engagements require a report to be issued
Both engagements provide limited assurance
A preparation engagement provides more assurance than a compilation
questions.explanation: A preparation engagement (AR-C 70) is a nonattest service that produces statements without a report, while a compilation (AR-C 80) results in a compilation report; neither provides assurance. Requiring a report for both, claiming assurance, or ranking preparation above compilation are incorrect.

questions.stem: Under the conceptual framework approach of the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct, a member should:
choices: Follow only the explicit rules and ignore situations the rules do not address
Identify threats to compliance with the rules, evaluate their significance, and apply safeguards to eliminate or reduce them to an acceptable level
Rely on management's judgment about the member's independence
Apply safeguards only after a violation has occurred
questions.explanation: The conceptual framework directs members to identify threats, evaluate their significance, and apply safeguards to reduce them to an acceptable level when the Code does not address a situation directly. Ignoring unaddressed situations, deferring to management, or waiting until after a violation are all inconsistent with the framework.

questions.stem: A member prepares source data and accounting records for a client and then audits those same records. This situation primarily creates a:
choices: Advocacy threat
Familiarity threat
Self-review threat
Undue influence threat
questions.explanation: Auditing one's own prior work, such as records the member prepared, is a self-review threat. An advocacy threat involves promoting a client's position, a familiarity threat arises from a close relationship, and an undue influence threat stems from pressure or dominance by the client.

questions.stem: Independence is required for which of the following engagements?
choices: Compilations
Preparation engagements
Tax return preparation
Audits and reviews (attest engagements)
questions.explanation: Independence is required for attest engagements such as audits and reviews. It is not required for compilations, preparation engagements, or nonattest services like tax preparation, although those still require integrity and objectivity.

questions.stem: How does a direct financial interest in an attest client affect a covered member's independence?
choices: Any direct financial interest impairs independence, regardless of the amount
A direct interest impairs independence only if it is material
A direct interest never impairs independence
A direct interest is evaluated the same way as an indirect interest
questions.explanation: A direct financial interest impairs a covered member's independence regardless of materiality, whereas an indirect interest impairs independence only if it is material. Treating a direct interest as material-dependent, harmless, or identical to an indirect interest is incorrect.

questions.stem: An indirect financial interest in an attest client (for example, an investment held through a diversified mutual fund):
choices: Always impairs independence, regardless of materiality
Impairs a covered member's independence only if the interest is material to the member
Never affects independence under any circumstances
Is treated identically to a direct financial interest
questions.explanation: An indirect financial interest impairs independence only when it is material to the covered member. A direct interest, by contrast, impairs independence regardless of amount, so the two are not evaluated the same way.

questions.stem: When a member performs nonattest services (such as bookkeeping) for an attest client, independence is preserved only if:
choices: The member makes all management decisions for the client
Nonattest services are assumed never to affect independence
Management takes responsibility for the results, designates a suitable individual to oversee the service, and the member does not assume management responsibilities
The member performs any nonattest service without safeguards
questions.explanation: To keep independence when performing nonattest services, management must accept responsibility for the results, designate a competent individual to oversee the service, and the member must not assume management responsibilities. Making management decisions, ignoring the effect on independence, or forgoing safeguards would impair independence.

questions.stem: Contingent fees and commissions from a client are:
choices: Always permitted for any client
Permitted for audit clients as long as they are disclosed
Required by the AICPA Code
Prohibited when the member also performs an audit or review for that client
questions.explanation: A member may not charge a contingent fee to, or accept a commission from, a client for whom the member performs an audit or a review (or certain other attest services). Contingent fees and commissions are not always permitted, are not cured by disclosure for attest clients, and are not required.

questions.stem: Under the confidential client information rule, a member:
choices: Must not disclose confidential client information without the client's consent, subject to limited exceptions such as a valid subpoena or a peer review
May disclose client information freely to third parties
Is bound by confidentiality only on tax engagements
May sell client information to competitors
questions.explanation: The confidential client information rule bars disclosure without the client's consent, with limited exceptions such as a legally enforceable subpoena, a peer review, or a professional inquiry. Free disclosure, limiting the rule to tax work, or selling client information all violate the rule.

questions.stem: The integrity and objectivity rule requires members to:
choices: Follow management's preferred accounting even when it is incorrect
Remain free of conflicts of interest, not knowingly misrepresent facts, and not subordinate their judgment to others
Advocate for the client's position in all matters
Subordinate their judgment to the audit committee
questions.explanation: The rule requires members to avoid conflicts of interest, refrain from knowingly misrepresenting facts, and not subordinate their professional judgment. Deferring to management's incorrect treatment, unconditional advocacy, or subordinating judgment to the audit committee all violate the rule.

questions.stem: Compared with the AICPA independence rules, the SEC and PCAOB independence requirements for issuers:
choices: Are identical to the AICPA rules for nonissuers
Eliminate independence requirements for issuers

Impose additional requirements, including audit partner rotation, audit committee pre-approval of services, restrictions on certain nonaudit services, and a cooling-off period before a former audit team member joins the client in a key role
Apply only to the engagement partner's spouse
questions.explanation: For issuers, the SEC and PCAOB add requirements such as audit partner rotation, audit committee pre-approval of services, prohibitions on specified nonaudit services, and a cooling-off period for former audit team members. These rules are stricter than, not identical to, the AICPA rules, do not eliminate independence, and are not limited to a spouse.

questions.stem: Loans between a covered member and an attest client:
choices: Never affect independence
Are always permitted as long as they are disclosed
Impair independence only when the client is an issuer
Generally impair independence, except for certain permitted loans such as specified consumer loans obtained under normal lending terms
questions.explanation: Loans between a covered member and an attest client generally impair independence, with narrow exceptions for certain consumer loans (for example, auto loans or credit-card balances within limits) made under normal terms. Loans are not uniformly harmless, disclosure does not cure them, and the rule is not limited to issuers.

questions.stem: With respect to family relationships and independence:
choices: Family relationships never affect a member's independence
Only the member's own financial interests matter for independence
A covered member's independence can be impaired when an immediate family member holds a key position (such as a role with financial reporting oversight) at the attest client
Independence is impaired only if a distant relative works in any capacity at the client
questions.explanation: A covered member's independence can be impaired by an immediate family member's employment in a key position at the attest client, because such positions influence financial reporting. Family relationships can matter, the member's own interests are not the only consideration, and the concern centers on key positions rather than any job held by a distant relative.

questions.stem: To qualify for head of household filing status, a taxpayer must generally:
choices: Be unmarried (or considered unmarried), pay more than half the cost of keeping up a home, and have a qualifying person live with them for more than half the year
Be married filing separately with no dependents
Simply have the highest income in the household
Claim any relative as a dependent regardless of where that relative lives
questions.explanation: Head of household requires the taxpayer to be unmarried or considered unmarried, furnish more than half the cost of maintaining the household, and have a qualifying person live with them for more than half the year. A dependent parent is a special exception who need not live with the taxpayer. The other options describe different statuses or misstate the rule.

questions.stem: A qualifying surviving spouse (qualifying widow(er)) may:
choices: File a joint return with the deceased spouse indefinitely
Use the joint-return tax rates and standard deduction for up to two years after the year of a spouse's death if they maintain a household for a dependent child and have not remarried
Only file as single beginning in the year of the spouse's death
Use this status only if there are no children
questions.explanation: A qualifying surviving spouse may use joint-return rates and the joint standard deduction for the two years following the year of the spouse's death, provided the taxpayer maintains a home for a dependent child and has not remarried; a joint return is still permitted in the year of death itself. The other options misstate the duration and conditions.

questions.stem: The tests for a qualifying child include:
choices: A gross income test limiting the child's income
The child must be under age 13 in all cases
Relationship, age, residency, and support, and generally not filing a joint return (except to claim a refund)
The child must provide more than half of their own support
questions.explanation: A qualifying child must satisfy the relationship, age, residency, and support tests and generally must not file a joint return. There is no gross income test for a qualifying child (that test applies to a qualifying relative), the age limits are broader than under 13, and a qualifying child must not have provided more than half of their own support.

questions.stem: Which of the following is included in gross income?
choices: A cash gift received from a relative
An inheritance received from an estate
Child support received
Gambling winnings
questions.explanation: Gross income includes all income from whatever source derived unless specifically excluded, so gambling winnings are taxable. Gifts, inheritances, and child support received are excluded from the recipient's gross income.

questions.stem: Property received as a gift or inheritance is:
choices: Excluded from the recipient's gross income, though income later earned on the property is taxable
Fully taxable to the recipient
Taxable only to the extent it exceeds $10,000
Deductible by the donor
questions.explanation: Property received by gift or inheritance is excluded from the recipient's gross income, although income subsequently generated by that property is taxable. It is not fully taxable, is not subject to a recipient threshold, and a gift is not deductible by the donor.

questions.stem: For federal income tax purposes:
choices: Interest on both municipal bonds and U.S. Treasury bonds is excluded from gross income
Interest on state and local government bonds is generally excluded from federal gross income, while interest on U.S. Treasury obligations is taxable federally
Interest on both municipal bonds and Treasury bonds is fully taxable federally
Municipal bond interest is taxable federally, but Treasury interest is exempt
questions.explanation: Interest on state and municipal bonds is generally excluded from federal gross income, whereas interest on federal obligations such as Treasury bonds is federally taxable (though exempt from state tax). The other options misstate which interest is exempt.

questions.stem: For a degree candidate, a scholarship is treated as follows:
choices: The entire scholarship is always tax-free
The entire scholarship is always taxable
Amounts used for tuition and required fees, books, and supplies are excluded, but amounts used for room and board are taxable
The scholarship is deductible by the student
questions.explanation: A degree candidate may exclude scholarship amounts used for tuition and required fees, books, and supplies, but amounts used for room, board, or other living expenses are taxable, as are amounts representing payment for services. The other options overstate or understate the exclusion.

questions.stem: How are Social Security retirement benefits treated for income tax purposes?
choices: Social Security benefits are always fully excluded from gross income
Social Security benefits are always fully taxable
Exactly 50% of Social Security benefits is always taxable
Up to 85% of benefits may be included in gross income depending on the taxpayer's provisional (combined) income
questions.explanation: The taxable portion of Social Security benefits ranges from 0% up to a maximum of 85%, determined by provisional income (modified AGI plus tax-exempt interest plus one-half of the benefits) relative to base thresholds. Benefits are neither always excluded, always fully taxable, nor fixed at 50%.

questions.stem: Under current law, the tax treatment of alimony is:
choices: For agreements executed after 2018, alimony is neither deductible by the payer nor included in the recipient's income; for pre-2019 agreements, it is deductible by the payer and included by the recipient
Alimony is always deductible by the payer
Alimony is always included in the recipient's income
Child support is deductible by the payer
questions.explanation: Under the TCJA, for divorce or separation agreements executed after December 31, 2018, alimony is not deductible by the payer and not taxable to the recipient; agreements executed before 2019 keep the prior deductible/includible treatment. Child support is never deductible or taxable.

questions.stem: Cancellation of debt (COD) income is:
choices: Always excluded from gross income
Generally included in gross income, but excluded to the extent the taxpayer is insolvent or the debt is discharged in bankruptcy
Always fully taxable regardless of the taxpayer's circumstances
Deductible by the debtor
questions.explanation: Cancellation of debt income is generally taxable, but exclusions apply for a discharge in bankruptcy and to the extent of insolvency (and for certain qualified debts). It is not always excluded, not always fully taxable, and not a deduction.

questions.stem: Employer-provided group-term life insurance is treated as follows for the employee:
choices: All employer-provided life insurance is fully taxable to the employee
All of it is fully excluded regardless of the amount of coverage
The cost of the first $50,000 of coverage is excluded, and the cost of coverage above that amount is included in income
It is deductible by the employee
questions.explanation: An employee excludes the cost of the first $50,000 of employer-provided group-term life insurance coverage; the cost of coverage exceeding $50,000 is included in income using an IRS premium table. It is neither fully taxable, fully excluded, nor a deduction to the employee.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an adjustment to income (a deduction taken in arriving at AGI) rather than an itemized deduction?
choices: Home mortgage interest
State income taxes
Charitable contributions
One-half of self-employment tax
questions.explanation: Adjustments (above-the-line deductions) reduce gross income to arrive at AGI and include items such as one-half of self-employment tax; mortgage interest, state income taxes, and charitable contributions are itemized (from-AGI) deductions. Distinguishing for-AGI from from-AGI deductions is a core individual-tax skill.

questions.stem: A self-employed taxpayer may:
choices: Deduct one-half of the self-employment tax as an adjustment to income
Deduct the full self-employment tax as an itemized deduction
Claim a credit for the entire self-employment tax
Exclude self-employment income from gross income
questions.explanation: A self-employed taxpayer deducts one-half of the self-employment tax as an adjustment in arriving at AGI, reflecting the employer-equivalent portion. The full tax is not itemized, there is no full credit, and self-employment income is not excluded.

questions.stem: Which statement about traditional and Roth IRA contributions is correct?
choices: Both traditional and Roth contributions are always deductible
Traditional IRA contributions may be deductible (subject to active-participation and AGI limits), while Roth IRA contributions are never deductible but qualified distributions are tax-free
Neither traditional nor Roth contributions are ever deductible
Roth contributions are deductible but traditional contributions are not
questions.explanation: Traditional IRA contributions may be deductible, with the deduction phased out for active participants in an employer plan above certain AGI levels, whereas Roth contributions are made with after-tax dollars (not deductible) and qualified distributions are tax-free. The other options misstate deductibility.

questions.stem: Interest paid on a qualified education loan is:
choices: An itemized deduction only
A tax credit
An adjustment to income (above-the-line), subject to a dollar cap and an AGI phase-out
Fully deductible with no limit
questions.explanation: Interest on a qualified education loan is deductible as an adjustment (above the line), subject to an annual dollar limit and a phase-out based on modified AGI. It is not an itemized deduction, not a credit, and not unlimited. (Verify the current dollar cap and phase-out range to the exam-year figures.)

questions.stem: In choosing between the standard deduction and itemized deductions, a taxpayer:
choices: Must always take the standard deduction
Must always itemize deductions
Adds the standard deduction to total itemized deductions
Claims the greater of the standard deduction or total itemized deductions, with an additional standard deduction amount for taxpayers who are 65 or older or blind
questions.explanation: A taxpayer deducts the greater of the standard deduction or itemized deductions, and taxpayers who are age 65 or older or blind receive an additional standard deduction amount. The two are alternatives, not additive, and neither is mandatory.

questions.stem: Unreimbursed medical expenses are:
choices: Deductible as an itemized deduction only to the extent they exceed 7.5% of adjusted gross income
Fully deductible with no floor
Deductible as an adjustment to income
Deductible only if the taxpayer is self-employed
questions.explanation: Unreimbursed medical expenses are itemized deductions to the extent they exceed 7.5% of AGI. They are not fully deductible, are not an above-the-line adjustment (self-employed health insurance is a separate provision), and are not limited to the self-employed.

questions.stem: The state and local tax (SALT) itemized deduction consists of:
choices: Unlimited state and local taxes of every kind
State and local income (or sales) taxes plus property taxes, deductible as an itemized deduction subject to an overall dollar cap
Federal income taxes paid
An adjustment to income
questions.explanation: The SALT itemized deduction covers state and local income taxes (or, by election, sales taxes) plus real and personal property taxes, subject to an overall dollar cap. Federal income taxes are never deductible, the deduction is capped rather than unlimited, and SALT is a from-AGI itemized deduction. (Verify the current SALT cap, which has been subject to legislative change.)

questions.stem: With respect to interest expense of an individual:
choices: Personal credit card interest is deductible as an itemized deduction
All interest paid by an individual is deductible
Qualified residence interest on acquisition indebtedness (within a debt limit) is deductible, and investment interest expense is deductible only up to net investment income
Investment interest expense is deductible without limit
questions.explanation: Individuals may itemize qualified residence interest on acquisition debt up to a statutory limit, and investment interest expense is limited to net investment income (with the excess carried forward). Personal (consumer) interest is nondeductible, not all interest is deductible, and investment interest is not unlimited.

questions.stem: Which statement about the charitable contribution deduction is correct?
choices: Charitable contributions are deductible without any AGI limit
All donated property is deductible at the donor's cost basis
No carryforward is allowed for contributions that exceed the annual limit

Cash gifts to public charities are deductible up to a percentage-of-AGI limit, gifts of long-term appreciated property are generally deductible at fair market value subject to a lower AGI limit, and excess contributions carry forward for five years
questions.explanation: Charitable deductions are subject to AGI percentage ceilings that vary by the type of gift and donee, long-term capital gain property is generally deducted at fair market value (subject to a lower ceiling), and contributions exceeding the ceilings carry forward for five years. Contributions are not unlimited, appreciated property is not limited to basis, and a carryforward is allowed. (Verify the current AGI percentage limits.)

questions.stem: Under current law, with respect to certain itemized deductions:
choices: Personal casualty losses are deductible only if attributable to a federally declared disaster, and miscellaneous itemized deductions subject to the 2%-of-AGI floor are suspended
All personal casualty losses are deductible
Unreimbursed employee business expenses are fully deductible
Tax preparation fees are deductible as a miscellaneous itemized deduction
questions.explanation: Under the TCJA, personal casualty and theft losses are deductible only when attributable to a federally declared disaster, and the category of miscellaneous itemized deductions subject to the 2%-of-AGI floor (such as unreimbursed employee expenses and tax preparation fees) is suspended. The other options describe pre-TCJA treatment.

questions.stem: The qualified business income (QBI) deduction is best described as:
choices: A 20% deduction available to C corporations

A deduction of up to 20% of qualified business income from pass-through entities and sole proprietorships, taken in arriving at taxable income (not as an itemized deduction and without reducing AGI), and limited above a taxable-income threshold by W-2 wage/property limits and rules for specified service businesses
An itemized deduction that reduces AGI
A credit equal to 20% of business income
questions.explanation: The QBI deduction equals up to 20% of qualified business income from pass-throughs and sole proprietorships and is subtracted in computing taxable income (it is neither an adjustment to AGI nor an itemized deduction). Above the taxable-income threshold, it is subject to W-2 wage and unadjusted-basis (UBIA) limits and is reduced or eliminated for specified service trades or businesses (SSTBs). It does not apply to C corporations and is not a credit. (Verify the current taxable-income threshold.)

questions.stem: The difference between a refundable and a nonrefundable tax credit is that:
choices: Both types can generate a refund beyond the tax owed
Both types can only reduce tax to zero
A refundable credit can reduce the tax below zero and generate a refund, while a nonrefundable credit can reduce tax only to zero
A nonrefundable credit is always more valuable than a refundable credit
questions.explanation: A refundable credit (such as the earned income credit) can reduce tax below zero and produce a refund of the excess, whereas a nonrefundable credit (such as the child and dependent care credit) can only reduce the tax liability to zero. The other statements misstate the distinction.

questions.stem: Comparing the American Opportunity Tax Credit (AOTC) and the Lifetime Learning Credit (LLC):
choices: Both credits are fully refundable
Both credits are limited to the first four years of college
A taxpayer may claim both credits for the same student in the same year

The AOTC applies per eligible student for the first four years of postsecondary education and is 40% refundable, while the LLC is computed per return, covers a broader range of education, and is nonrefundable
questions.explanation: The American Opportunity credit is claimed per student, limited to the first four years of postsecondary education, and is 40% refundable, whereas the Lifetime Learning credit is figured per tax return, applies to a wider range of coursework, and is nonrefundable. Both credits cannot be claimed for the same student in the same year, and only the AOTC is partially refundable and year-limited.

questions.stem: High-income individuals may be subject to:
choices: A 3.8% additional tax on all wages
A 0.9% tax on all investment income

An additional 0.9% Medicare tax on wages and self-employment income above a threshold, and a 3.8% net investment income tax on the lesser of net investment income or the excess of modified AGI over a threshold
Both taxes, but only if they are corporations
questions.explanation: High-income individuals may owe an additional 0.9% Medicare tax on earned income (wages and self-employment income) above a threshold and a 3.8% net investment income tax on the lesser of net investment income or the amount by which modified AGI exceeds a threshold. The rates and bases in the other options are incorrect, and these are individual (not corporate) taxes.

questions.stem: The cost basis of purchased property generally includes:
choices: The purchase price plus costs to acquire the asset and place it in service (such as sales tax, freight, and installation)
Only the cash paid, excluding any acquisition costs
The fair market value at the end of the year
The seller's original cost of the asset
questions.explanation: Cost basis is the purchase price plus the costs necessary to acquire the asset and place it in service, such as sales tax, freight, and installation. It is not limited to cash paid, is not year-end fair market value, and is not the seller's basis.

questions.stem: For determining gain on the later sale of property received as a gift:
choices: The donee's basis is always the fair market value at the date of the gift
The donor's adjusted basis carries over to the donee, and the donee's holding period includes the donor's holding period
The donee's basis is zero
The donee's holding period always begins on the date of the gift
questions.explanation: For gain, a donee takes the donor's adjusted basis (carryover basis) and tacks on the donor's holding period. Fair market value is used only in the loss branch of the double-basis rule, the basis is not zero, and the holding period tacks for gain.

questions.stem: For determining a loss on property received as a gift, the donee's basis is:
choices: Always the donor's adjusted basis
Always the fair market value at the date of the gift, regardless of the donor's basis
The lower of the donor's adjusted basis or the fair market value at the date of the gift
Never available, because a donee can never recognize a loss on gifted property
questions.explanation: Under the double-basis rule, loss basis is the lower of the donor's adjusted basis or the fair market value on the gift date. When the sale price falls between the gain basis and the loss basis, neither gain nor loss is recognized. Using only the donor's basis, only fair market value, or barring losses entirely is incorrect.

questions.stem: The basis of inherited property is generally:
choices: The decedent's original cost
The fair market value at the date the decedent purchased it
Zero
The fair market value at the date of the decedent's death (or the alternate valuation date if elected), with an automatically long-term holding period
questions.explanation: Inherited property takes a basis equal to fair market value at the date of death (or the alternate valuation date, generally six months later, if elected), and it is automatically long-term regardless of actual holding time. The decedent's cost, purchase-date fair market value, and a zero basis are all incorrect.

questions.stem: A taxpayer's adjusted basis in an asset is:
choices: Increased by capital improvements and decreased by depreciation or cost recovery deductions
Never changed after acquisition
Increased by routine repairs and decreased by capital improvements
Equal to the asset's fair market value each year
questions.explanation: Adjusted basis rises with capital improvements and falls with depreciation and other cost recovery. Basis does change over time, routine repairs are expensed rather than capitalized, and basis is not marked to fair market value.

questions.stem: For a capital gain or loss to be long-term, the asset must be held:
choices: More than six months
More than one year
More than two years
Exactly one year
questions.explanation: An asset must be held more than one year for the resulting gain or loss to be long-term; a holding period of one year or less is short-term. Six months, two years, and exactly one year are incorrect thresholds.

questions.stem: When a loss is disallowed under the wash sale rule:
choices: The disallowed loss is permanently lost
The loss is deductible in full in the year of sale
The disallowed loss is added to the basis of the replacement securities, and the holding period of the replacement includes that of the securities sold
The replacement's basis equals its purchase price with no adjustment
questions.explanation: In a wash sale the disallowed loss increases the basis of the replacement stock (preserving it for the future) and the holding period tacks. The loss is deferred rather than permanently lost or currently deductible, and the replacement basis is adjusted upward by the disallowed loss.

questions.stem: Which statement about realized and recognized gain is correct?
choices: Realized and recognized gain are always the same amount
Recognized gain is always higher than realized gain
Gain is recognized only when cash is received
Realized gain equals the amount realized minus the adjusted basis, and it is recognized (taxed) unless a nonrecognition provision applies
questions.explanation: Realized gain is the amount realized less adjusted basis, and it is generally recognized unless a specific nonrecognition rule (like-kind exchange, involuntary conversion, etc.) defers it. Realized and recognized amounts can differ, recognized never exceeds realized, and noncash consideration can still trigger recognition.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a capital asset?
choices: Inventory held for sale, accounts receivable from operations, and depreciable property used in a trade or business
All property owned by a taxpayer is a capital asset
Personal-use property is never a capital asset
Stocks and bonds held for investment are not capital assets
questions.explanation: A capital asset is defined by exclusion: inventory, business accounts receivable, and depreciable or real property used in a trade or business (Section 1231 assets) are not capital assets. Not all property is capital, personal-use assets are capital assets, and investment stocks and bonds are capital assets.

questions.stem: An individual's net capital loss is:
choices: Fully deductible against ordinary income
Deductible against ordinary income only up to $3,000 per year, with the excess carried forward indefinitely
Not deductible at all
Deductible against ordinary income, with the excess carried back three years
questions.explanation: Individuals may offset up to $3,000 of net capital loss against ordinary income each year, and any excess is carried forward indefinitely, retaining its short- or long-term character. The loss is neither fully deductible nor wholly disallowed, and individuals do not carry capital losses back.

questions.stem: Net long-term capital gain of an individual is:
choices: Taxed at ordinary income tax rates
Exempt from tax
Generally taxed at preferential rates lower than the rates on ordinary income
Taxed at preferential rates only if it is short-term
questions.explanation: Net long-term capital gain (from assets held more than one year) is taxed at preferential rates (0%, 15%, or 20%, depending on taxable income), while short-term gain is taxed at ordinary rates. Capital gain is not exempt, and short-term gain does not receive the preferential rate.

questions.stem: How are net Section 1231 gains and losses characterized?
choices: Both net Section 1231 gains and losses are ordinary
Both net Section 1231 gains and losses are capital
A net Section 1231 gain is ordinary and a net Section 1231 loss is capital
A net Section 1231 gain is treated as long-term capital gain, while a net Section 1231 loss is treated as an ordinary loss
questions.explanation: Section 1231 provides favorable treatment: a net gain is long-term capital gain (preferential rates), while a net loss is ordinary (fully deductible). A look-back rule can recharacterize gain as ordinary to the extent of prior unrecaptured Section 1231 losses. The other options misstate this treatment.

questions.stem: On the sale of depreciable personal property at a gain, Section 1245:
choices: Recaptures the gain as ordinary income to the extent of the depreciation previously taken, with any remaining gain treated as Section 1231 gain
Treats all of the gain as capital gain
Applies to real property rather than personal property
Converts the gain into tax-exempt income
questions.explanation: Section 1245 recaptures prior depreciation on personal property as ordinary income up to the amount of the gain, and any excess gain is Section 1231 gain. Not all such gain is capital, Section 1245 applies to personal (not real) property, and recapture does not create exempt income.

questions.stem: For an individual selling depreciable real property at a gain, which statement is correct?
choices: All of the gain on real property is recaptured as ordinary income under Section 1250
The portion of the gain attributable to prior straight-line depreciation (unrecaptured Section 1250 gain) is taxed at a maximum rate of 25%
Section 1250 gain is always tax-free
Gain on real property is always short-term
questions.explanation: For real property depreciated using straight-line, there is generally no ordinary recapture under Section 1250, but the portion of gain attributable to that depreciation, called unrecaptured Section 1250 gain, is taxed at a maximum 25% rate for individuals. It is not fully ordinary, not tax-free, and not short-term.

questions.stem: Under current law, Section 1031 like-kind exchange treatment:
choices: Applies to exchanges of any property, including personal property and inventory
Requires immediate recognition of all gain
Defers gain or loss on the exchange of real property held for productive use in a trade or business or for investment for like-kind real property
Applies only to exchanges of corporate stock
questions.explanation: After the TCJA, Section 1031 nonrecognition applies only to exchanges of real property held for business or investment; it no longer covers personal property. Inventory and stock never qualified, and the purpose of the rule is to defer, not immediately recognize, gain.

questions.stem: When a taxpayer receives boot in an otherwise qualifying like-kind exchange:
choices: All realized gain is recognized because boot was received
Boot has no effect on the recognition of gain
Recognized gain equals the boot received even if it exceeds the realized gain
Recognized gain equals the lesser of the realized gain or the boot (non-like-kind property, such as cash) received
questions.explanation: Receiving boot triggers recognition of gain equal to the lesser of the realized gain or the boot received; a loss is never recognized even when boot is received. Recognizing all realized gain, ignoring boot, or recognizing boot in excess of realized gain are all incorrect.

questions.stem: Under Section 1033, gain from an involuntary conversion (such as a casualty or condemnation):
choices: May be deferred if the taxpayer reinvests the proceeds in qualifying replacement property within the required time period
Must always be recognized immediately
Results in an automatic deferral of any loss
May be deferred even if no replacement property is acquired
questions.explanation: Under Section 1033, gain from an involuntary conversion may be deferred to the extent the proceeds are reinvested in qualifying replacement property within the statutory period. Gain is not automatically recognized, the provision addresses gains rather than deferring losses, and reinvestment is required for deferral.

questions.stem: The Section 121 exclusion on the sale of a principal residence allows an individual to:
choices: Exclude all gain on the sale of any home
Exclude up to $250,000 ($500,000 for married filing jointly) of gain if the ownership and use tests are met (owned and used as a main home for at least two of the last five years)
Exclude gain on the sale of rental and investment property
Claim the exclusion on an unlimited number of home sales every year
questions.explanation: Section 121 excludes up to $250,000 of gain ($500,000 for joint filers) on the sale of a principal residence when the two-of-five-year ownership and use tests are satisfied; the exclusion is generally available only once every two years. It does not cover all homes, rental/investment property, or unlimited annual sales. (Verify the exclusion amounts to current law.)

questions.stem: The wash sale rule disallows a loss when the taxpayer:
choices: Acquires a substantially identical security within 60 days after the sale only
Reacquires the securities in the same calendar year
Acquires a substantially identical security within 30 days before or after the sale
Realizes a gain rather than a loss on the sale
questions.explanation: The wash sale rule disallows a loss when substantially identical securities are purchased within the 61-day window (30 days before through 30 days after the sale). It is not limited to 60 days after or to the same calendar year, and it disallows losses, not gains.

questions.stem: Regarding a loss on the sale of property to a related party under Section 267:
choices: The loss is fully deductible
Gains on sales to related parties are exempt from tax
The disallowed loss is permanently lost to both parties with no relief
The loss is disallowed, but the related buyer may reduce the gain on a later sale to an unrelated party by the previously disallowed loss
questions.explanation: Section 267 disallows a loss on a sale to a related party, but the related purchaser can use the disallowed loss to reduce gain when the property is later sold to an unrelated party (the right of offset). The loss is not currently deductible, related-party gains are not exempt, and the disallowed loss is not entirely without relief.

questions.stem: A loss on qualifying small business stock under Section 1244 is:
choices: Treated as an ordinary loss up to an annual limit, rather than as a capital loss
Treated as an ordinary loss without any limit
A source of tax-free gains
Never deductible
questions.explanation: Section 1244 allows an original holder to treat a loss on qualifying small business stock as an ordinary loss (up to $50,000, or $100,000 on a joint return), with any excess treated as a capital loss. Not all stock losses are ordinary, Section 1244 addresses losses rather than tax-free gains, and the loss is deductible. (Verify the ordinary-loss limit to current law.)

questions.stem: Under MACRS, the recovery periods for real property are:
choices: All real property is depreciated over 15 years
Residential rental real property over 27.5 years and nonresidential real property over 39 years, while most machinery and equipment use 5- or 7-year periods
Residential rental property over 39 years and nonresidential real property over 27.5 years
Land is depreciated over 27.5 years
questions.explanation: Under MACRS, residential rental property has a 27.5-year recovery period and nonresidential real property a 39-year period, while personal property such as equipment is 5- or 7-year. A 15-year period for all real property, the reversed periods, and depreciating land (which is never depreciated) are all incorrect.

questions.stem: Which statement about MACRS conventions is correct?
choices: All property uses the mid-month convention
All property uses the half-year convention regardless of when it is placed in service

Personal property generally uses the half-year convention, switching to the mid-quarter convention if more than 40% of such property is placed in service in the last quarter, and real property uses the mid-month convention
Real property uses the half-year convention
questions.explanation: MACRS applies the half-year convention to personal property, the mid-quarter convention when more than 40% of personal property is placed in service in the fourth quarter, and the mid-month convention to real property. The other options misapply the conventions.

questions.stem: The Section 179 deduction is:
choices: Mandatory expensing of all asset purchases
A deduction with no dollar limit
Available only to C corporations
An election to expense the cost of qualifying property up to an annual dollar limit, with the limit phased out above an investment threshold and the deduction limited to taxable business income
questions.explanation: Section 179 lets a taxpayer elect to immediately expense the cost of qualifying property up to an annual limit; the limit is reduced dollar-for-dollar once purchases exceed an investment threshold, and the deduction cannot exceed taxable income from the business (with a carryforward of the excess). It is elective, capped, and available to various taxpayers, not just C corporations. (Verify the current dollar limit and investment threshold.)

questions.stem: Acquired intangible assets under Section 197, such as goodwill and covenants not to compete, are:
choices: Never amortizable for tax purposes
Amortized over 5 years
Amortized on a straight-line basis over 15 years
Expensed immediately in the year of acquisition
questions.explanation: Section 197 requires acquired intangibles, including goodwill, going-concern value, franchises, and covenants not to compete, to be amortized straight-line over 15 years regardless of their actual useful life. Goodwill is amortizable for tax (unlike book), the period is 15 years (not 5), and the cost is not immediately expensed.

questions.stem: Orr Company, a calendar-year corporation, reported net income of $500,000 for Year 1. Throughout Year 1 it had 100,000 shares of common stock and 10,000 shares of 6%, $100 par preferred stock outstanding. No dividends were declared in Year 1. What is Year 1 basic earnings per share?
questions.explanation: Preferred stock is noncumulative unless the stem says 'cumulative.' For noncumulative preferred, dividends reduce the EPS numerator only if declared; none were declared, so basic EPS = 500,000 / 100,000 = $5.00. $4.40 subtracts the $60,000 annual preferred dividend as if it were cumulative. $5.60 adds it (wrong direction). $4.70 subtracts only a partial dividend.

questions.stem: Pell Corporation accounts for treasury stock under the cost method. It reissued 400 treasury shares that had cost $30 per share for $22 per share. Immediately before the reissuance, additional paid-in capital from treasury stock had a $2,000 balance. By what amount does this reissuance reduce retained earnings?
questions.explanation: Cost method is the default. Reissuing below cost creates a $3,200 shortfall (12,000 cost − 8,800 proceeds) that reduces APIC–treasury stock first ($2,000), with the remaining $1,200 charged to retained earnings — never to income. $3,200 ignores the APIC–T/S balance. $0 wrongly books the loss to the income statement. $2,000 is only the APIC–T/S portion.

questions.stem: Sten Company, a lessee, enters a five-year equipment lease. The rate implicit in the lease is readily determinable and equals 6%. Sten's incremental borrowing rate is 8%. Sten is not eligible for any private-company elections. At which rate should Sten discount the payments to measure the lease liability?
choices: 8%, the incremental borrowing rate
7%, the average of the two rates
6%, the rate implicit in the lease
A risk-free rate for a comparable term
questions.explanation: The lessee discount-rate hierarchy is: rate implicit in the lease (if determinable) → incremental borrowing rate → risk-free rate (private-company election). Because the implicit rate is determinable, Sten uses 6%. The IBR is only a fallback, not a choice. Averaging is never permitted. The risk-free election is unavailable here and applies only when the implicit rate is not determinable.

questions.stem: Dax Company purchased equipment for $120,000 on October 1, Year 1. The equipment has a four-year useful life and is depreciated straight-line. The reporting period ends December 31. What is depreciation expense for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Salvage value is assumed to be zero when not stated, so the annual straight-line charge is 120,000 / 4 = $30,000. The asset was held only three months in Year 1 (Oct 1–Dec 31): 30,000 × 3/12 = $7,500. $30,000 ignores the partial period. $22,500 uses nine months (counts in the wrong direction). $10,000 miscounts the partial period as four months.

questions.stem: Quill Company incurred the following during Year 1 on a project to develop a new product: research staff salaries $400,000; equipment purchased solely for this project with no alternative future use $250,000; and routine quality-control testing of existing products $60,000. What amount should Quill expense as research and development for Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP, R&D is expensed as incurred. Equipment with no alternative future use is expensed in full as R&D (only equipment with alternative future use is capitalized and depreciated). Routine quality control is a production cost, not R&D. R&D = 400,000 + 250,000 = $650,000. $400,000 wrongly capitalizes the special-purpose equipment. $710,000 includes quality control. $460,000 makes both errors.

questions.stem: At December 31, Year 1, Brae Company held: currency on hand $10,000; a Treasury bill purchased November 1, Year 1 maturing January 30, Year 2 (original maturity 90 days) $50,000; a Treasury bill purchased six months earlier with a six-month original maturity, now one month from maturity $40,000; and a 90-day certificate of deposit purchased in December $20,000. What amount should Brae report as cash and cash equivalents?
questions.explanation: A cash equivalent must have an original maturity of three months or less from the date of purchase. The $40,000 bill had a six-month original maturity, so it is not a cash equivalent even though only one month remains. Cash and cash equivalents = 10,000 + 50,000 + 20,000 = $80,000. $120,000 uses remaining maturity. $60,000 wrongly excludes the qualifying 90-day CD. $10,000 counts currency only.

questions.stem: Vance Company contracts to build equipment for a fixed price of $500,000 plus a $50,000 bonus if completed by a target date. Vance concludes it is not highly probable that a significant reversal of the bonus will not occur. What transaction price should Vance use?
questions.explanation: Variable consideration is included only to the extent it is highly probable that a significant reversal will not occur (the constraint). The bonus fails that test, so it is excluded: transaction price = $500,000. $550,000 ignores the constraint. $525,000 includes half. $450,000 wrongly reduces the fixed price.

questions.stem: Mott operates a platform that arranges services performed by independent providers. Mott does not control the services before they transfer to customers. During Year 1, customers paid $100,000; Mott remitted $85,000 to providers and retained $15,000. What amount of revenue should Mott recognize?
questions.explanation: An entity that arranges for another party to provide goods or services, and does not control them before transfer, is an agent and recognizes revenue net of amounts paid to the provider: $15,000. $100,000 treats Mott as a principal (gross). $85,000 is the providers' share. $115,000 double-counts.

questions.stem: Pard Company issued $500,000 of five-year, 6% bonds paying interest semiannually. The bonds were issued for $463,202 to yield 8% compounded semiannually. The bond agreement does not specify an amortization method. What is interest expense for the first six-month interest period?
questions.explanation: The effective-interest method is the default. Interest expense = carrying amount × semiannual yield = 463,202 × 4% = $18,528. $15,000 is the cash coupon (500,000 × 3%), which ignores amortization. $20,000 applies the yield to face value instead of carrying amount. $37,056 uses the annual rate for a six-month period.

questions.stem: Crane Company, a lessee, signs a five-year lease requiring payments of $50,000 at the end of each year. The rate implicit in the lease is determinable and is 6%; Crane's incremental borrowing rate is 8%. The present value of an ordinary annuity of $1 for five periods is 4.2124 at 6% and 3.9927 at 8%. What amount should Crane record as the initial lease liability?
questions.explanation: Discount at the implicit rate when it is determinable: 50,000 × 4.2124 = $210,620. $199,635 uses the IBR (50,000 × 3.9927), a fallback only. $223,257 treats the payments as an annuity due. $250,000 fails to discount.

questions.stem: Tine Company uses FIFO. At year-end it holds 1,000 units of a product with a unit cost of $48. The product sells for $60 per unit; estimated costs to complete are $12 per unit and costs to sell are $3 per unit. At what amount should the inventory be reported?
questions.explanation: Non-LIFO inventory is measured at the lower of cost and net realizable value. NRV = 60 − 12 − 3 = $45 per unit, which is below the $48 cost, so inventory is written down to 1,000 × 45 = $45,000. $48,000 keeps cost (no write-down). $57,000 deducts only the selling cost and forgets the completion cost. $60,000 uses the selling price.

questions.stem: Lure Company overstated its December 31, Year 1 ending inventory by $20,000. The error is discovered in Year 3. Ignoring income taxes, what is the effect on net income for Year 1 and Year 2?
choices: Overstated in both Year 1 and Year 2
Understated in Year 1; overstated in Year 2
No effect on either year
Overstated in Year 1; understated in Year 2
questions.explanation: Overstated ending inventory understates cost of goods sold, so Year 1 net income is overstated. That ending balance becomes Year 2 beginning inventory, overstating Year 2 COGS and understating Year 2 net income — the error counterbalances over two years. 'Both overstated' ignores the reversal; the second option reverses the direction; 'no effect' ignores the impact entirely.

questions.stem: On January 1, Year 1, Saxe Company acquired 30% of the voting common stock of Vee Company for $400,000, giving Saxe significant influence. During Year 1, Vee reported net income of $200,000 and paid cash dividends of $50,000. What amount should Saxe report as its investment in Vee at December 31, Year 1?
questions.explanation: Under the equity method (20–50% ownership), the investment increases by the investor's share of investee net income (+60,000) and decreases by its share of dividends (−15,000): 400,000 + 60,000 − 15,000 = $445,000. $460,000 omits the dividend reduction (treats dividends as income). $415,000 records dividends but not income. $400,000 leaves the investment at cost.

questions.stem: Poe Company holds equity securities representing a 5% interest in another entity, with no significant influence. The securities cost $100,000 and have a fair value of $120,000 at year-end. How should the $20,000 increase in fair value be reported?
choices: In other comprehensive income
In net income
Not recognized until the securities are sold
As a direct adjustment to retained earnings
questions.explanation: Equity securities without significant influence are measured at fair value through net income; the $20,000 unrealized gain goes to net income. Routing equity-security fair-value changes to OCI is the superseded available-for-sale treatment (eliminated). Deferring recognition until sale and adjusting retained earnings directly are both incorrect.

questions.stem: Yarn Company holds debt securities appropriately classified as available-for-sale. The securities cost $200,000. At December 31, Year 1, their fair value was $185,000 and Yarn recognized the resulting unrealized loss. At December 31, Year 2, their fair value was $172,000. There is no expected credit loss. What unrealized loss should Yarn report in other comprehensive income for Year 2?
questions.explanation: For AFS debt, unrealized gains and losses accumulate in OCI; the current-year amount is the change in the cumulative unrealized position. Cumulative loss = 200,000 − 172,000 = 28,000; $15,000 was recognized in Year 1, so Year 2 OCI loss = $13,000. $28,000 is the cumulative figure. $15,000 repeats the prior year. $0 recognizes nothing.

questions.stem: Stent Company has a taxable temporary difference of $100,000 at December 31, Year 1 that is expected to reverse in future years. The enacted tax rate is 30% for Year 1 and 21% for all future years. What amount should Stent report as a deferred tax liability at December 31, Year 1?
questions.explanation: Deferred tax balances are measured at the enacted tax rate expected to apply when the difference reverses — 21% — so the DTL = 100,000 × 21% = $21,000. $30,000 uses the current-period rate. $25,500 averages the two rates. $0 wrongly treats the item as a permanent difference.

questions.stem: Roth Company earned $40,000 of interest on municipal bonds during the year. The interest is included in financial-statement income but is never taxable. This item gives rise to:
choices: No deferred tax — it is a permanent difference
A deferred tax asset
A deferred tax liability
A valuation allowance
questions.explanation: Items that affect book income but never affect taxable income (or vice versa) are permanent differences and create no deferred tax. Deferred tax assets and liabilities arise only from temporary differences that reverse in future periods. A valuation allowance addresses a DTA's realizability, which is not at issue here.
