questions.stem: Gray Company sold a single delivery truck used in operations for more than its carrying amount. The truck is not a component of the entity. How should Gray report this transaction in its income statement?
choices: At gross, with proceeds in revenues and the carrying amount in expenses
At the net gain, within continuing operations
At the net gain, within other comprehensive income
At the net gain, within discontinued operations
questions.explanation: Gains and losses on disposals of operating assets are reported net within continuing operations. Gross presentation applies to ordinary revenues and expenses, not asset disposals. OCI excludes such gains. Discontinued-operations treatment requires disposal of a component representing a strategic shift — a single asset does not qualify.

questions.stem: Under U.S. GAAP, how should a company classify cash dividends paid to shareholders and cash interest paid on debt in the statement of cash flows?
choices: Both as financing activities
Both as operating activities
Dividends paid as financing; interest paid as operating
Dividends paid as operating; interest paid as financing
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP, dividends paid to owners are financing activities, while interest paid is an operating activity (it enters the determination of net income). The other combinations mix up these required classifications. (IFRS permits more flexibility, but U.S. GAAP is the default.)

questions.stem: Nilo Company reported net income of $300,000 for Year 1. Net income includes depreciation expense of $40,000 and a $25,000 gain on the sale of equipment (the equipment was sold for $90,000 cash). Assuming no changes in working-capital accounts, what is net cash provided by operating activities under the indirect method?
questions.explanation: The indirect method starts with net income, adds back non-cash depreciation (+40,000), and removes the gain (−25,000) because the full $90,000 proceeds belong in investing: 300,000 + 40,000 − 25,000 = $315,000. $365,000 adds the gain (wrong direction). $340,000 omits the gain adjustment. $405,000 puts the sale proceeds in operating.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Welk Company account for a sale of goods for which no over-time criterion is met?
choices: Recognize revenue at the point in time control transfers
Recognize revenue over time as costs are incurred
Defer all revenue until cash is collected
Recognize revenue when the contract is signed
questions.explanation: When none of the three over-time criteria are met, the obligation is satisfied at a point in time, so recognize revenue when control transfers. Indicators of control transfer: physical possession, legal title, risks and rewards, customer acceptance, and a present right to payment. Cash collection and contract signing are not, by themselves, control-transfer events.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Marr Company account for a service the customer simultaneously receives and consumes as the entity performs?
choices: Recognize revenue at completion
Recognize revenue over time
Recognize revenue when invoiced
Defer until the customer confirms satisfaction
questions.explanation: This is the first over-time criterion: the customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefits as the entity performs (routine or recurring services such as cleaning or transaction processing). Recognize revenue over time using a measure of progress. Invoice date and completion date do not drive recognition here.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Oste Company account for construction of an asset the customer controls as it is built?
choices: Recognize revenue at delivery
Recognize revenue when title transfers
Recognize revenue over time
Recognize revenue only upon final inspection
questions.explanation: Second over-time criterion: the customer controls the asset as it is created or enhanced (for example, building on land the customer owns). Recognize revenue over time by a measure of progress. Title transfer or final inspection is not the trigger when the customer already controls the work in progress.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Penn Company account for an asset with no alternative use to the entity plus an enforceable right to payment for performance to date?
choices: Recognize revenue at the point of delivery
Defer revenue until the asset is sold to a third party
Recognize revenue when production begins
Recognize revenue over time
questions.explanation: Third over-time criterion: the asset has no alternative use to the seller AND the seller has an enforceable right to payment for performance completed to date. Both conditions must hold. When they do, recognize revenue over time using a progress measure, not at delivery and not when production merely begins.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Quade Company account for variable consideration such as a performance bonus or rebate?
choices: Estimate it and include only the amount highly probable not to reverse
Include the full amount regardless of reversal risk
Exclude it entirely until it is received
Recognize it only when the cash is collected
questions.explanation: Estimate variable consideration using the expected value or the most-likely-amount method, then apply the constraint: include it only to the extent it is highly probable that a significant revenue reversal will not occur. Including the full amount ignores the constraint; excluding it entirely until cash arrives understates revenue when a reliable estimate exists.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Tarn Company account for an arrangement where the entity arranges for a third party to provide the good and does not control it before transfer?
choices: Recognize revenue gross, as a principal
Recognize revenue net, as an agent
Recognize no revenue at all
Recognize revenue equal to the amount paid to the supplier
questions.explanation: Control before transfer is the deciding test. If the entity only arranges for another party to provide the good or service and does not control it before transfer, it is an agent and recognizes only the net fee or commission. A principal, which controls the good first, recognizes the gross amount.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Volk Company account for sales taxes collected from customers on behalf of the government?
choices: Include them in revenue
Recognize them as other income
Exclude them from the transaction price
Defer them as a contract liability
questions.explanation: Amounts collected on behalf of third parties, such as sales and use taxes remitted to the government, are not the entity's consideration, so exclude them from the transaction price and from revenue. They are a payable to the taxing authority, not other income and not a contract liability.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Wren Company account for goods shipped to a consignee under a consignment arrangement?
choices: Recognize revenue when the goods are shipped to the consignee
Recognize revenue when the consignee receives the goods
Recognize revenue ratably over the consignment period
Recognize no revenue until the consignee sells to an end customer
questions.explanation: In a consignment the consignor keeps control until the consignee sells to a third party, so shipping to the consignee is not a sale. Recognize revenue (and derecognize inventory) only when the end customer buys. Consignment indicators: the product is returnable, the consignor can require its return or transfer, and the consignee has no unconditional obligation to pay for it.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Yale Company account for a bill-and-hold arrangement?
choices: Recognize revenue only if the strict bill-and-hold criteria are met
Recognize revenue when payment is received
Never recognize revenue until physical delivery
Recognize revenue when the order is placed
questions.explanation: Bill-and-hold can recognize revenue before shipment only if ALL four criteria hold: the reason is substantive (e.g., the customer asked for it), the goods are separately identified as the customer's, they are ready for physical transfer, and the seller cannot use them or redirect them to another customer. Otherwise control has not transferred. Receiving payment or taking the order does not transfer control, and a blanket 'never until delivery' rule ignores the exception.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Zorn Company account for a contract that fails the 'probable collection' criterion?
choices: Recognize revenue and a large allowance
Conclude no contract exists and recognize no revenue
Recognize revenue over the expected collection period
Recognize revenue net of estimated bad debts
questions.explanation: Collectibility is a Step 1 gate in ASC 606. If it is not probable the entity will collect the consideration it is entitled to, no contract exists yet, so no revenue is recognized; any cash received sits as a deposit/liability until the criteria are met or the cash is nonrefundable and performance is done. You do not gross up revenue with an allowance, that conflates a Step 1 failure with ordinary bad-debt estimation.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Ames Company account for allocation of a bundled transaction price among multiple performance obligations?
choices: Allocate equally to each obligation
Allocate entirely to the obligation delivered first
Allocate based on relative standalone selling prices
Allocate based on the entity's cost of each obligation
questions.explanation: Step 4 allocates the transaction price to each performance obligation in proportion to its standalone selling price (SSP). When SSP is not directly observable, estimate it (adjusted market assessment, expected cost plus a margin, or the residual approach). Equal splits and cost-based splits are not permitted, and you do not load the entire price onto whatever is delivered first.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Birk Company account for a product sold with a right of return?
choices: Recognize the full sales amount with no adjustment
Defer all revenue until the return period expires
Recognize revenue only for items not returned, when known
Recognize revenue net of expected returns, with a refund liability and return asset
questions.explanation: A right of return is variable consideration. Recognize revenue only for the amount you expect to keep, set up a refund liability for expected returns, and record a return asset (the former carrying amount of goods, less expected recovery cost). Recognizing the full sale ignores the variability; deferring everything understates revenue when returns are reasonably estimable.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Kerr Company account for a bill-and-hold arrangement?
choices: Recognize revenue only if the strict bill-and-hold criteria are met
Recognize revenue when payment is received
Never recognize revenue until physical delivery
Recognize revenue when the order is placed
questions.explanation: Bill-and-hold can recognize revenue before shipment only if ALL four criteria hold: the reason is substantive (e.g., the customer asked for it), the goods are separately identified as the customer's, they are ready for physical transfer, and the seller cannot use them or redirect them to another customer. Otherwise control has not transferred. Receiving payment or taking the order does not transfer control, and a blanket 'never until delivery' rule ignores the exception.

questions.stem: Under ASC 606, how should Poe Company account for a bill-and-hold arrangement?
choices: Recognize revenue only if the strict bill-and-hold criteria are met
Recognize revenue when payment is received
Never recognize revenue until physical delivery
Recognize revenue when the order is placed
questions.explanation: Bill-and-hold can recognize revenue before shipment only if ALL four criteria hold: the reason is substantive (e.g., the customer asked for it), the goods are separately identified as the customer's, they are ready for physical transfer, and the seller cannot use them or redirect them to another customer. Otherwise control has not transferred. Receiving payment or taking the order does not transfer control, and a blanket 'never until delivery' rule ignores the exception.

questions.stem: For Pare Company, a lessee, a lease in which ownership of the asset transfers to the lessee by the end of the term is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
An operating lease
A short-term lease expensed straight-line
A direct-financing lease
questions.explanation: Transfer of ownership to the lessee by the end of the lease term is the first finance-lease criterion. Meeting any single criterion makes it a finance lease (front-loaded interest plus separate amortization of the right-of-use asset), not an operating lease.

questions.stem: For Shel Company, a lessee, a lease in which the lease contains a purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise is classified as:
choices: An operating lease
A finance lease
A short-term lease
A service contract
questions.explanation: A purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise is the second finance-lease criterion, so the lease is a finance lease. 'Reasonably certain' is a high threshold driven by economic incentive (such as a bargain price). It is not an operating or short-term lease, and a lease conveying control of an identified asset is not a mere service contract.

questions.stem: For Welk Company, a lessee, a lease in which the lease term is for the major part of the asset's remaining economic life is classified as:
choices: An operating lease
A short-term lease
A finance lease
An executory contract
questions.explanation: The third criterion is that the lease term is for the major part of the asset's remaining economic life (commonly assessed around a 75% guideline). Meeting it makes the lease a finance lease, not an operating or executory contract.

questions.stem: For Marr Company, a lessee, a lease in which the present value of the payments is substantially all of the asset's fair value is classified as:
choices: An operating lease
A short-term lease
A month-to-month lease
A finance lease
questions.explanation: The fourth criterion is that the present value of the lease payments, plus any residual value the lessee guarantees, equals substantially all of the asset's fair value (commonly around 90% or more). Meeting it classifies the lease as a finance lease.

questions.stem: For Oste Company, a lessee, a lease in which the asset is so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor at the end of the term is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
An operating lease
A short-term lease
A sale-leaseback
questions.explanation: The fifth criterion is that the underlying asset is so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor at the end of the term. Meeting it classifies the lease as a finance lease.

questions.stem: For Penn Company, a lessee, a lease in which a lease in which none of the five finance-lease criteria are met is classified as:
choices: A finance lease
An operating lease
A sales-type lease
A direct-financing lease
questions.explanation: A lessee classifies a lease as a finance lease if ANY one of five criteria is met: transfer of ownership, a purchase option reasonably certain to be exercised, lease term covering the major part of the asset's remaining economic life, present value of payments equal to substantially all of fair value, or an asset so specialized it has no alternative use to the lessor. If none are met, it is an operating lease, still on balance sheet as a right-of-use asset and lease liability, but with a single straight-line lease expense.

questions.stem: For Quade Company, a lessee, a lease in which a lease of 12 months or less with no purchase option is classified as:
choices: Always a finance lease
Always capitalized at present value
Eligible for the short-term lease election (expense payments straight-line)
Never recognized in the financial statements
questions.explanation: A lease of 12 months or less with no purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise qualifies for the short-term lease policy election: skip the right-of-use asset and lease liability and recognize the payments straight-line over the term. It is not automatically a finance lease, and it is still reflected in expense.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Jode Company and its subsidiary, intercompany receivables and payables between a parent and subsidiary is:
choices: Eliminated only to the extent of the parent's ownership
Eliminated in full, regardless of the ownership percentage
Reported as related-party balances
Netted against noncontrolling interest
questions.explanation: Intercompany receivables and payables are eliminated in full in consolidation, even when the subsidiary is only partially owned, because the consolidated entity cannot owe money to itself. The noncontrolling-interest percentage affects the allocation of equity and income, not whether intra-group balances are removed.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Kerr Company and its subsidiary, the noncontrolling interest at the acquisition date under U.S. GAAP is:
choices: Measured at the parent's cost
Measured at the NCI's share of book value
Measured at fair value
Not recognized until the subsidiary is sold
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP, noncontrolling interest is measured at fair value on the acquisition date (the full-goodwill approach), and it is recognized immediately as a component of consolidated equity. It is not the parent's cost and not merely the NCI's share of book value.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Lund Company and its subsidiary, acquisition-related legal, advisory, and due-diligence costs is:
choices: Capitalized as part of the consideration transferred
Added to goodwill
Recorded as a reduction of equity
Expensed as incurred
questions.explanation: Under the acquisition method, direct acquisition costs (legal, advisory, due-diligence) are expensed as incurred. They are not part of consideration transferred and do not affect goodwill. (Costs to issue debt or equity are handled under their own guidance, separate from this rule.)

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Mabe Company and its subsidiary, the accounting method required for a business combination is:
choices: The acquisition method
The pooling-of-interests method
The equity method
The proportionate consolidation method
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP, noncontrolling interest is measured at fair value on the acquisition date (the full-goodwill approach), and it is recognized immediately as a component of consolidated equity. It is not the parent's cost and not merely the NCI's share of book value.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Nuss Company and its subsidiary, unrealized profit in ending inventory from an intercompany sale is:
choices: Recognized immediately in consolidated income
Deferred until the inventory is sold to an outside party
Allocated entirely to noncontrolling interest
Ignored if the subsidiary is wholly owned
questions.explanation: Profit on intercompany inventory still on hand at year-end is unrealized to the group, so defer it until that inventory is sold to an outside party. It is not recognized immediately, and for upstream (subsidiary-to-parent) sales the deferral is shared between the controlling and noncontrolling interests.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Pace Company and its subsidiary, a gain on an intercompany sale of land is:
choices: Recognized in consolidated income in the year of sale
Recorded directly in equity
Deferred until the land is sold outside the consolidated group
Amortized over the land's remaining life
questions.explanation: Intercompany profit is not earned from the consolidated group's perspective until the asset leaves the group. A gain on an intercompany land sale is eliminated and deferred, then recognized only when the land is sold to an outside party. It is not recognized in the year of the intercompany sale, and it is never recorded directly in equity.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Rune Company and its subsidiary, an investment that conveys control (more than 50% of voting interest) is:
choices: Accounted for under the equity method
Reported at fair value through net income
Reported at amortized cost
Consolidated
questions.explanation: A controlling financial interest, generally more than 50% of voting interest absent other arrangements, requires consolidation: combine 100% of the subsidiary's assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses, and present any noncontrolling interest within equity. The equity method (significant influence, roughly 20-50%) and fair-value or amortized-cost reporting do not apply once control exists.

questions.stem: In preparing consolidated financial statements for Selk Company and its subsidiary, goodwill recognized in a business combination is:
choices: Consideration transferred + fair value of NCI − fair value of identifiable net assets
Consideration transferred − book value of net assets
The excess of book value over fair value of net assets
Consideration transferred + fair value of identifiable net assets
questions.explanation: Goodwill equals consideration transferred plus the fair value of any noncontrolling interest plus the fair value of any previously held equity interest, minus the fair value of identifiable net assets acquired. It is a residual measured against fair values, never book values.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, revenue recognition under the modified accrual basis is:
choices: When earned, regardless of collection
When measurable and available
When the budget is adopted
When cash is ultimately received, always
questions.explanation: Under modified accrual, governmental-fund revenue is recognized when it is both measurable and available, meaning collectible within the period or soon enough after year-end to pay current-period obligations. 'When earned regardless of collection' describes full accrual, not modified accrual.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, the 'available' criterion for property taxes is:
choices: Collectible within the next fiscal year
Collectible at any future date
Collectible within 60 days after year-end
Collectible within 90 days after year-end
questions.explanation: For property taxes under modified accrual, 'available' generally means collectible within 60 days after year-end. Amounts that do not meet the availability window are reported as a deferred inflow of resources rather than revenue.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, a capital asset purchased by a governmental fund is:
choices: Capitalized and depreciated in the fund
Reported as an other financing use
Recorded only in fiduciary funds
Reported as an expenditure of the fund (not capitalized in the fund)
questions.explanation: In a governmental fund (current financial resources focus, modified accrual), purchasing a capital asset is recorded as an expenditure; the asset is not capitalized or depreciated within the fund. It is capitalized and depreciated only in the government-wide statements.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, long-term debt issued by a governmental fund is:
choices: Netted against capital outlay expenditures in the fund statements
Recorded as an other financing source (the liability appears only government-wide)
Recorded as a fund liability of the governmental fund, just as it appears in the government-wide statements
Recorded as fund revenue in the period the debt proceeds are received
questions.explanation: Issuing long-term debt in a governmental fund is recorded as an other financing source, because it provides current financial resources. The long-term liability itself appears only in the government-wide statements, not as a fund liability.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, the fund balance categories, most-constrained to least is:
choices: Restricted, Unrestricted, Designated, Reserved, Free
Nonspendable, Restricted, Committed, Assigned, Unassigned
Reserved, Designated, Unreserved
Spendable, Nonspendable, Reserved, Free
questions.explanation: GASB 54 fund-balance categories, most-constrained to least, are Nonspendable, Restricted, Committed, Assigned, and Unassigned. The older reserved/designated/unreserved labels are obsolete.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, when encumbrances are recorded is:
choices: When the goods are received
When the invoice is paid
When a purchase commitment (such as a purchase order) is made
At the end of the fiscal year only
questions.explanation: An encumbrance is recorded when a purchase commitment is made, such as when a purchase order is issued, reserving budgetary appropriation. It is reversed when the actual expenditure is recorded upon receipt, not when the invoice is paid and not only at year-end.

questions.stem: In governmental fund accounting, the measurement focus and basis used by the government-wide statements is:
choices: Current financial resources focus, modified accrual
Cash basis
Budgetary basis
Economic resources focus, full accrual basis
questions.explanation: Government-wide statements use the economic resources measurement focus and the full accrual basis (like a business), reporting capital assets and long-term debt. Governmental funds, by contrast, use the current financial resources focus and modified accrual.

questions.stem: For Selk, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, donated services is treated as follows:
choices: Recognized only when the donor formally requests recognition and supplies a written fair-value estimate
Never recognized as contribution revenue, even when the services require specialized professional skills
Recognized for all volunteer hours at fair value, regardless of whether the services create a nonfinancial asset or require specialized skills
Recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills that would otherwise be purchased
questions.explanation: Donated services are recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills (for example an attorney, CPA, or electrician) that would otherwise have to be purchased. General volunteer hours that fail both tests are not recognized.

questions.stem: For Tove, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, a quid pro quo contribution where the donor receives a benefit is treated as follows:
choices: Contribution revenue equals the full amount paid
Contribution revenue equals the fair value of the benefit
No contribution revenue is recognized
Contribution revenue equals the amount paid minus the fair value of the benefit received
questions.explanation: A quid pro quo payment is part exchange and part gift. Contribution revenue equals the amount paid minus the fair value of the benefit the donor receives; the exchange portion is recognized under the exchange (ASC 606) model. Recording the full amount as contribution overstates the gift.

questions.stem: For Urne, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, a cost-reimbursement grant is treated as follows:
choices: Conditional — revenue is recognized as qualifying costs are incurred
Unconditional — revenue is recognized immediately
Recognized only when the grant is closed out
Recorded as a liability until the grant expires
questions.explanation: A cost-reimbursement grant contains a barrier (incurring qualifying costs) and a right of return, which makes it a conditional contribution: recognize revenue only as the qualifying costs are incurred. It is not unconditional, you do not wait for close-out, and you do not park the entire amount as a liability indefinitely.

questions.stem: For Vance, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, an unconditional pledge collectible over several years is treated as follows:
choices: Deferred entirely until the pledged cash is received, since multi-year pledges are not yet earned
Recognized only as each annual installment of the pledge arrives
Recorded as a conditional promise to give
Contribution revenue now at present value, net of an estimated uncollectible allowance
questions.explanation: An unconditional pledge is recognized as contribution revenue now, measured at the present value of the expected future cash flows and net of an allowance for uncollectible amounts; multi-year pledges are generally donor-restricted by time. You do not defer to cash receipt and you do not treat it as a conditional promise.

questions.stem: For Pard, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, the net asset classes a nongovernmental NFP reports is treated as follows:
choices: Unrestricted, temporarily restricted, permanently restricted
Restricted and unrestricted fund balances
With donor restrictions and without donor restrictions
Spendable and nonspendable
questions.explanation: Under current GAAP an NFP reports two net asset classes: with donor restrictions and without donor restrictions. The older three-bucket model (unrestricted, temporarily restricted, permanently restricted) was superseded, and governmental fund-balance terms do not apply to NFPs.

questions.stem: For Spin, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, donated services is treated as follows:
choices: Recognized for all volunteer hours at fair value, regardless of whether the services create a nonfinancial asset or require specialized skills
Never recognized as contribution revenue, even when the services require specialized professional skills
Recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills that would otherwise be purchased
Recognized only when the donor formally requests recognition and supplies a written fair-value estimate
questions.explanation: Donated services are recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills (for example an attorney, CPA, or electrician) that would otherwise have to be purchased. General volunteer hours that fail both tests are not recognized.

questions.stem: For Saxe, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, a cost-reimbursement grant is treated as follows:
choices: Unconditional — revenue is recognized immediately
Conditional — revenue is recognized as qualifying costs are incurred
Recognized only when the grant is closed out
Recorded as a liability until the grant expires
questions.explanation: A cost-reimbursement grant contains a barrier (incurring qualifying costs) and a right of return, which makes it a conditional contribution: recognize revenue only as the qualifying costs are incurred. It is not unconditional, you do not wait for close-out, and you do not park the entire amount as a liability indefinitely.

questions.stem: For Pare, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, donated services is treated as follows:
choices: Recognized for all volunteer hours at fair value, regardless of whether the services create a nonfinancial asset or require specialized skills
Recognized only when the donor formally requests recognition and supplies a written fair-value estimate
Never recognized as contribution revenue, even when the services require specialized professional skills
Recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills that would otherwise be purchased
questions.explanation: Donated services are recognized only if they create or enhance a nonfinancial asset, or require specialized skills (for example an attorney, CPA, or electrician) that would otherwise have to be purchased. General volunteer hours that fail both tests are not recognized.

questions.stem: For Welk, a nongovernmental not-for-profit organization, a cost-reimbursement grant is treated as follows:
choices: Unconditional — revenue is recognized immediately
Recognized only when the grant is closed out
Conditional — revenue is recognized as qualifying costs are incurred
Recorded as a liability until the grant expires
questions.explanation: A cost-reimbursement grant contains a barrier (incurring qualifying costs) and a right of return, which makes it a conditional contribution: recognize revenue only as the qualifying costs are incurred. It is not unconditional, you do not wait for close-out, and you do not park the entire amount as a liability indefinitely.

questions.stem: In partnership accounting, a noncash asset contributed by a partner is handled as:
choices: Recorded at the partner's carrying amount
Recorded at its fair value at the contribution date
Recorded at original cost
Recorded at the lower of cost or fair value
questions.explanation: Noncash assets contributed to a partnership are recorded at fair value on the contribution date, with the contributing partner's capital account credited for that fair value. The partner's old carrying amount, original cost, or lower-of-cost-or-fair-value are not used, because each partner's capital should reflect the economic value brought into the partnership.

questions.stem: In partnership accounting, the order of payment in a partnership liquidation is handled as:
choices: Partner capital, then loans, then creditors
Pro rata among all claimants
Outside creditors, then partner loans, then partner capital
Partner loans, then creditors, then capital
questions.explanation: Liquidation priority is outside (third-party) creditors first, then partner loans, then partner capital balances. Outside claimants are satisfied before partners recover anything they lent to or invested in the partnership.

questions.stem: In partnership accounting, the bonus method when an incoming partner pays more than book value for an interest is handled as:
choices: Goodwill is recorded for the excess paid above the book value of the partnership interest acquired
The excess is recognized immediately as income by the partnership
The excess is recorded as a liability owed to the incoming partner
The excess is allocated to the existing partners' capital; no goodwill is recorded
questions.explanation: Under the bonus method, total recorded capital equals the actual assets invested, so any premium the incoming partner pays above book value is shifted to the existing partners' capital accounts as a bonus. No goodwill (an unrecorded intangible) is created, that would be the goodwill method, which is generally disfavored.

questions.stem: In partnership accounting, allocation of partnership profit when salary and interest allowances exceed net income is handled as:
choices: Income is divided equally among the partners, regardless of the partnership agreement
Allowances are still allocated, and the resulting deficiency is shared in the loss ratio
No salary or interest allowances are granted in any year that partnership net income is insufficient
Allowances are prorated down to the amount of income actually available
questions.explanation: Salary and interest allowances are allocation devices, not expenses, so they are applied in full even when they exceed net income. The resulting negative residual is then divided among the partners in their profit-and-loss (residual) ratio. Income is not simply split equally or prorated to available income when the agreement specifies allowances.

questions.stem: Hale Company (the parent) acquired 70% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $600,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $210,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $600,000 + $210,000 − $700,000 = $110,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Jode Company (the parent) acquired 80% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $700,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $140,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $700,000 + $140,000 − $700,000 = $140,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Lund Company (the parent) acquired 75% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $700,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $175,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $700,000 + $175,000 − $700,000 = $175,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Spruce Company (the parent) acquired 90% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $800,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $70,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $700,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $800,000 + $70,000 − $700,000 = $170,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Oste Company (the parent) acquired 70% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $800,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $300,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $1,000,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $800,000 + $300,000 − $1,000,000 = $100,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Volk Company (the parent) acquired 80% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $900,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $200,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $1,000,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $900,000 + $200,000 − $1,000,000 = $100,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Ames Company (the parent) acquired 75% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $900,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $250,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $1,000,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $900,000 + $250,000 − $1,000,000 = $150,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Cole Company (the parent) acquired 90% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $900,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $80,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $800,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $900,000 + $80,000 − $800,000 = $180,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Dunn Company (the parent) acquired 90% of a subsidiary. Consideration transferred was $900,000, the fair value of the noncontrolling interest was $90,000, and the fair value of the subsidiary's identifiable net assets was $900,000. What is goodwill?
questions.explanation: Goodwill = consideration + FV of NCI − FV of identifiable net assets = $900,000 + $90,000 − $900,000 = $90,000. Distractors omit the NCI, use only the parent's share of net assets, or add net assets instead of subtracting.

questions.stem: Nuss Company is constructing a building for its own use. Weighted-average accumulated expenditures during the year were $400,000, total construction cost to date was $800,000, and the relevant interest rate was 10% (actual interest cost exceeded the computed amount). How much interest should be capitalized this year?
questions.explanation: Capitalized interest = weighted-average accumulated expenditures × rate = $400,000 × 10% = $40,000 (capped at actual interest, not binding here). Distractors apply the rate to total cost, omit the rate, or halve the result.

questions.stem: At year-end, the subsidiary of Goss Company still holds $30,000 (at transfer price) of inventory purchased from the parent, who sells to the subsidiary at 50% above cost. What unrealized intercompany profit must be eliminated in consolidation?
questions.explanation: Markup of 50% on cost equals a 33.3% margin on selling price; unrealized profit = $30,000 × 33.3% = $10,000. Distractors apply the markup rate to the selling price, defer the whole balance, or back into the cost portion.

questions.stem: Saxe Company signs a contract for a bundled price of $90,000 covering two performance obligations whose standalone selling prices are $40,000 and $60,000. How much of the transaction price is allocated to the first obligation?
questions.explanation: Allocate by relative standalone selling price: $90,000 × $40,000 / $100,000 = $36,000. Distractors use the standalone price without allocating the discount, allocate to the other obligation, or split the price equally.

questions.stem: Marr Company signs a contract for a bundled price of $108,000 covering two performance obligations whose standalone selling prices are $30,000 and $90,000. How much of the transaction price is allocated to the first obligation?
questions.explanation: Allocate by relative standalone selling price: $108,000 × $30,000 / $120,000 = $27,000. Distractors use the standalone price without allocating the discount, allocate to the other obligation, or split the price equally.

questions.stem: Quade Company signs a contract for a bundled price of $90,000 covering two performance obligations whose standalone selling prices are $80,000 and $20,000. How much of the transaction price is allocated to the first obligation?
questions.explanation: Allocate by relative standalone selling price: $90,000 × $80,000 / $100,000 = $72,000. Distractors use the standalone price without allocating the discount, allocate to the other obligation, or split the price equally.

questions.stem: Ames Company signs a contract for a bundled price of $90,000 covering two performance obligations whose standalone selling prices are $25,000 and $75,000. How much of the transaction price is allocated to the first obligation?
questions.explanation: Allocate by relative standalone selling price: $90,000 × $25,000 / $100,000 = $22,500. Distractors use the standalone price without allocating the discount, allocate to the other obligation, or split the price equally.

questions.stem: Goss Company signs a contract for a bundled price of $90,000 covering two performance obligations whose standalone selling prices are $45,000 and $55,000. How much of the transaction price is allocated to the first obligation?
questions.explanation: Allocate by relative standalone selling price: $90,000 × $45,000 / $100,000 = $40,500. Distractors use the standalone price without allocating the discount, allocate to the other obligation, or split the price equally.

questions.stem: A donor pays $150 to attend a fundraising gala held by Jode, a nongovernmental not-for-profit, and receives benefits with a fair value of $40. What amount should Jode record as contribution revenue?
questions.explanation: Contribution revenue = amount paid − fair value of the benefit received = $150 − $40 = $110. Distractors use the full amount, only the benefit, or the sum.

questions.stem: Tarn, a nongovernmental not-for-profit, receives unconditional pledges of $120,000 during the year and estimates that 10% will be uncollectible. What amount of contribution revenue should be recognized?
questions.explanation: Unconditional pledges are revenue now, net of the estimated uncollectible allowance: $120,000 × (1 − 10%) = $108,000. Distractors ignore the allowance, report only the allowance, or add it.

questions.stem: A company switches from the FIFO to the weighted-average inventory method. How is this change reported?
choices: Prospectively, affecting only the current and future periods
Retrospectively, restating prior periods and adjusting beginning retained earnings
As a discontinued operation, net of tax
As a prior-period adjustment to current-year net income only
questions.explanation: A change in accounting principle is applied retrospectively: prior-period statements presented are restated as if the new method had always been used, and the cumulative effect adjusts the earliest beginning retained earnings shown. (A change to LIFO is the practical exception, applied prospectively.)

questions.stem: A company revises the estimated useful life of equipment from 10 years to 7 years. How is this revision accounted for?
choices: Retrospectively restate prior depreciation
Prospectively over the asset's remaining life
As a correction of an error
Charge the full catch-up to current-year expense
questions.explanation: A change in accounting estimate is handled prospectively: spread the remaining book value over the new remaining useful life going forward. Estimates are not restated, because new information, not an error, drove the change.

questions.stem: During the current year a company discovers it failed to record depreciation in a prior year. How is this corrected?
choices: Prospectively as a change in estimate
As a component of current-year income from continuing operations
As a prior-period adjustment, restating the prior statements and beginning retained earnings
Disclosed in the notes only, with no statement adjustment
questions.explanation: Failing to record depreciation is an error. Correct it as a prior-period adjustment: restate the affected prior-period statements and adjust the opening balance of retained earnings (net of tax). Errors are never run through current-year income from continuing operations.

questions.stem: To report a disposal as a discontinued operation, the disposal must:
choices: Represent a strategic shift that has (or will have) a major effect on operations
Exceed ten percent of the entity's total assets at year end
Be any sale of a long-lived asset, regardless of whether it represents a strategic shift in operations
Result in a realized loss on the disposal of the component
questions.explanation: Under ASC 205-20, a disposal qualifies as a discontinued operation only if it represents a strategic shift with a major effect on the entity's operations and financial results. Qualifying results are reported separately, net of tax, below income from continuing operations.

questions.stem: Which item is reported in other comprehensive income rather than in net income?
choices: Gain on sale of equipment
Unrealized gain on an available-for-sale debt security
Cost of goods sold
Interest expense
questions.explanation: Unrealized holding gains and losses on available-for-sale debt securities bypass net income and are reported in OCI until realized. Realized gains, COGS, and interest expense all flow through net income.

questions.stem: A material lawsuit is filed against a company after the balance-sheet date but before the financial statements are issued, arising from an incident that occurred after year-end. This is:
choices: A recognized (Type I) subsequent event requiring adjustment
A nonrecognized (Type II) subsequent event requiring disclosure only
Ignored entirely
A prior-period adjustment
questions.explanation: The triggering incident happened after the balance-sheet date, so no condition existed at year-end. That makes it a nonrecognized (Type II) subsequent event: do not adjust the statements, but disclose it if material. A Type I event reflects a condition that already existed at the balance-sheet date and requires adjustment.

questions.stem: Under segment reporting, an operating segment is generally reportable if its reported revenue, absolute profit or loss, or assets are at least what share of the combined totals?
questions.explanation: ASC 280 uses 10% quantitative thresholds: a segment is reportable if its revenue (internal + external), the absolute value of its profit or loss, or its assets are 10% or more of the relevant combined amount. A separate test ensures reportable segments total at least 75% of external revenue.

questions.stem: On the balance sheet of a nonmonetary exchange that lacks commercial substance, the acquired asset is generally recorded at:
choices: The acquirer's estimated replacement cost for the asset
Fair value of the asset received, with the full gain or loss recognized immediately in earnings
Zero, until cash consideration is actually paid
The book value of the asset given up (with limited gain only if boot is received)
questions.explanation: When an exchange lacks commercial substance, it is recorded at the carrying amount of the asset surrendered, and gains are generally deferred. A partial gain is recognized only to the extent of boot (cash) received. Exchanges with commercial substance, by contrast, use fair value and recognize full gain or loss.

questions.stem: Under U.S. GAAP, cash paid for interest on debt is classified in the statement of cash flows as:
choices: Operating
Investing
Financing
A noncash item
questions.explanation: Under U.S. GAAP, interest paid and interest/dividends received are operating cash flows. Only dividends PAID are financing. (IFRS allows more flexibility, but the FAR default is interest paid = operating.)

questions.stem: Using the indirect method, which adjustment is made to net income to derive operating cash flow?
choices: Subtract a decrease in inventory from net income for the period
Add depreciation expense and subtract an increase in accounts receivable
Subtract depreciation expense and add an increase in accounts receivable to net income
Add an increase in accounts receivable directly to reported net income
questions.explanation: Indirect method: add back noncash expenses like depreciation, and reverse working-capital changes. An increase in a current asset (AR) uses cash, so it is subtracted; an increase in a current liability provides cash, so it is added.

questions.stem: Cash received from selling a piece of equipment is reported in which section of the statement of cash flows?
choices: Operating
Investing
Financing
Not reported
questions.explanation: Buying or selling long-lived productive assets is an investing activity, so proceeds from selling equipment appear in investing. The gain or loss on the sale is removed from operating under the indirect method to avoid double counting.

questions.stem: Issuing common stock for cash and later paying a cash dividend are reported, respectively, as:
choices: Operating and operating
Financing and financing
Financing and operating
Investing and financing
questions.explanation: Transactions with the entity's own owners are financing activities: issuing stock is a financing inflow and paying dividends is a financing outflow. (Note the asymmetry with interest, which is operating under U.S. GAAP.)

questions.stem: Why does U.S. GAAP require the allowance method rather than the direct write-off method for uncollectible accounts?
choices: It matches bad-debt expense to the period of the related sale and states receivables at net realizable value
It avoids estimating uncollectible amounts and recognizes a loss only when a specific account actually defaults
It is simpler to apply because no estimate of uncollectible accounts is ever required
It is permitted only for income-tax reporting, not for financial-statement purposes
questions.explanation: The allowance method estimates uncollectibles in the same period as the related revenue (matching) and reports receivables at net realizable value. The direct write-off method, which waits until a specific account is deemed worthless, violates matching and overstates receivables, so GAAP disallows it.

questions.stem: A company factors its receivables WITHOUT recourse. This transaction is accounted for as:
choices: A secured borrowing, keeping the receivables on the books
A sale, removing the receivables and recognizing any gain or loss
A contingent liability only
An equity transaction
questions.explanation: Factoring without recourse transfers the risk of non-collection to the buyer, so the seller derecognizes the receivables and records a sale (with a loss/financing fee). Factoring WITH recourse, where the seller retains collection risk, is typically treated as a secured borrowing.

questions.stem: A noninterest-bearing note receivable due in three years is initially recorded at:
choices: Its present value, discounted at the market rate, with the difference recognized as interest over time
Its face amount reduced by a fixed ten-percent allowance for imputed interest
Zero until the note is collected, when the full amount is recorded as interest income
Its face (maturity) amount, since a noninterest-bearing note carries no stated rate and therefore no discount
questions.explanation: A long-term note that bears no (or an unreasonably low) stated rate is recorded at present value using the market/imputed rate. The discount is amortized to interest income over the note's life, so the carrying amount accretes toward face value.

questions.stem: Under the current expected credit loss (CECL) model, the allowance for credit losses on financial assets reflects:
choices: Only the credit losses already incurred and probable at the balance sheet date, under the old incurred-loss model
Expected credit losses over the asset's contractual life, using past, current, and reasonable forecasted information
A fixed one percent of the outstanding receivable balance applied uniformly each period
Credit losses recognized only once an account becomes ninety days past due
questions.explanation: CECL (ASC 326) is a forward-looking model: recognize a lifetime expected-credit-loss allowance at origination, incorporating historical experience, current conditions, and reasonable, supportable forecasts. It replaced the older 'incurred loss' approach that waited until a loss was probable.

questions.stem: A company using FIFO measures inventory at the lower of cost and:
choices: Replacement cost
Net realizable value (estimated selling price less costs to complete and sell)
Original cost
Net realizable value less a normal profit margin
questions.explanation: For inventory measured with FIFO or weighted average, the rule is lower of cost and net realizable value (NRV = selling price minus costs to complete and sell). The older ceiling/floor 'market' computation (replacement cost bounded by NRV and NRV-less-margin) now applies only to LIFO and retail-method inventory.

questions.stem: Goods shipped FOB shipping point on the last day of the year are in transit at year-end. Which party includes them in inventory?
choices: The seller
The buyer
Neither party
Both parties, split 50/50
questions.explanation: FOB shipping point transfers title (and risk) to the buyer when the goods leave the seller's dock, so the buyer owns goods in transit and includes them in inventory. Under FOB destination, the seller retains title until delivery.

questions.stem: During a period of rising prices, which inventory method generally produces the HIGHEST ending inventory and net income?
choices: LIFO
FIFO
Weighted average
Specific identification
questions.explanation: With rising prices, FIFO leaves the newest, highest costs in ending inventory and expenses the oldest, lowest costs as COGS, producing higher ending inventory and higher net income. LIFO does the opposite (lower income, lower taxes).

questions.stem: A company estimates ending inventory for interim reporting using the gross profit method. If sales are $500,000 and the historical gross profit rate is 40%, estimated cost of goods sold is:
questions.explanation: Gross profit method: COGS = sales × (1 − gross profit rate) = 500,000 × (1 − 0.40) = $300,000. Estimated ending inventory is then goods available for sale minus this estimated COGS.

questions.stem: Research and development costs incurred internally are generally:
choices: Capitalized and amortized over 40 years
Expensed as incurred
Capitalized as goodwill
Recorded in other comprehensive income
questions.explanation: U.S. GAAP requires most R&D costs to be expensed as incurred, because future benefits are too uncertain at that stage. (Once technological feasibility is established, certain software development costs may be capitalized.)

questions.stem: An intangible asset with an indefinite useful life (e.g., a renewable broadcast license) is:
choices: Not amortized, but tested for impairment at least annually
Expensed in full immediately in the period it is acquired
Amortized over a maximum allowable period of forty years
Amortized on a straight-line basis over a ten-year statutory period
questions.explanation: Indefinite-life intangibles are not amortized; instead they are tested for impairment at least annually (and when events indicate possible impairment). Finite-life intangibles are amortized over their useful lives.

questions.stem: To test a finite-lived long-lived asset (held for use) for impairment, the first step is to compare the carrying amount with:
choices: The asset's current replacement cost
The asset's original acquisition cost
The asset's current fair value measured using market-participant assumptions
The sum of the undiscounted future cash flows expected from the asset
questions.explanation: Step 1 is a recoverability test: if the carrying amount exceeds the undiscounted expected future cash flows, the asset is impaired. Step 2 then measures the loss as carrying amount minus fair value. Undiscounted cash flows screen for impairment; fair value sizes it.

questions.stem: Under current U.S. GAAP, goodwill impairment for a reporting unit is measured as:
choices: The excess of the reporting unit's carrying amount over its fair value, limited to the goodwill balance
The excess of implied goodwill over carrying goodwill in a two-step test
Always the full goodwill balance
Amortization over 10 years
questions.explanation: After ASU 2017-04, goodwill impairment is a single-step test: the loss equals the amount by which the reporting unit's carrying amount exceeds its fair value, capped at the goodwill recorded. The old Step 2 (computing implied goodwill) was eliminated. Internally generated goodwill is never recorded.

questions.stem: Unrealized holding gains and losses on TRADING debt securities are reported in:
choices: Other comprehensive income
Net income (earnings)
Retained earnings directly
Not recognized until sale
questions.explanation: Trading securities are measured at fair value with unrealized gains and losses recognized in net income each period. Available-for-sale debt securities, by contrast, route unrealized changes through OCI.

questions.stem: Held-to-maturity debt securities are reported on the balance sheet at:
choices: Fair value, changes to OCI
Fair value, changes to net income
Amortized cost
Lower of cost or market
questions.explanation: Debt securities the entity has the positive intent and ability to hold to maturity are carried at amortized cost (no fair-value marking), with premium/discount amortized to interest income. Only debt can be HTM; equity cannot.

questions.stem: Under ASU 2016-01, an equity investment with a readily determinable fair value (not consolidated and without significant influence) is measured at:
choices: Cost less impairment
Fair value with changes recognized in net income
Fair value with changes in OCI
Amortized cost
questions.explanation: Since ASU 2016-01, such equity investments are measured at fair value through net income; the old available-for-sale (OCI) treatment for equity securities was eliminated. The remaining OCI-eligible category is AFS debt securities.

questions.stem: Which set of items is reported in other comprehensive income (the 'PUFI' items)?
choices: Pension adjustments, unrealized AFS debt gains, foreign-currency translation, and instrument-specific credit risk
Accounts payable, unsecured debt, finance leases, and interest expense
Prepaid expenses, unearned revenue, fixed assets, and inventory
Profit on sales, undistributed retained earnings, fixed manufacturing overhead costs, and interest paid to lenders during the year
questions.explanation: PUFI is a memory aid for common OCI items: Pension funded-status changes, Unrealized gains/losses on AFS debt securities, Foreign-currency translation adjustments, and changes in fair value attributable to Instrument-specific credit risk for certain liabilities under the fair-value option. These bypass net income until reclassified.

questions.stem: Which item is excluded from 'cash and cash equivalents'?
choices: A 60-day Treasury bill purchased near maturity
A money-market fund
Equity securities held for trading
Coin and currency on hand
questions.explanation: Cash equivalents are short-term, highly liquid investments with an original maturity of three months or less (e.g., T-bills, money-market funds). Equity securities are not cash equivalents because their value fluctuates and they have no fixed maturity.

questions.stem: In a bank reconciliation, an outstanding check (recorded by the company but not yet cleared by the bank) is:
choices: Added to the bank balance
Subtracted from the bank balance
Added to the book balance
Subtracted from the book balance
questions.explanation: Outstanding checks have been recorded by the company but have not reduced the bank's balance yet, so they are subtracted from the bank balance to reconcile. Deposits in transit are added to the bank balance; bank charges and NSF items adjust the book balance.

questions.stem: A bond is issued when the market (effective) rate exceeds the stated (coupon) rate. The bond sells at:
choices: A premium
A discount
Par
Face plus accrued interest only
questions.explanation: When investors demand a higher yield than the coupon pays, they will only buy the bond for less than face — a discount. The discount is amortized over the bond's life, increasing interest expense above the cash coupon. (Stated > market would produce a premium.)

questions.stem: Under the effective-interest method, periodic interest expense on a bond issued at a discount equals:
choices: The cash coupon payment
The carrying amount times the market rate
The face amount times the stated rate
A straight-line portion of total interest
questions.explanation: Effective-interest expense = beginning carrying amount × market (effective) rate. For a discount bond this exceeds the cash coupon, and the difference increases the carrying amount toward face value. The effective-interest method is GAAP-preferred over straight-line.

questions.stem: As a bond issued at a premium is amortized using the effective-interest method, interest expense each period:
choices: Increases
Decreases
Stays equal to the cash coupon
Equals zero
questions.explanation: For a premium bond, the carrying amount declines toward face value over time, and since interest expense = carrying amount × market rate, expense decreases each period. Premium amortization reduces interest expense below the cash coupon.

questions.stem: Under the cost method, a company reacquires its own shares as treasury stock. The reacquisition is recorded by:
choices: Debiting treasury stock at the reacquisition cost, reducing total equity
Recognizing a gain or loss on the reacquisition in current net income
Reducing common stock at its par value and adjusting paid-in capital accordingly
Increasing retained earnings by the cost of the reacquired shares
questions.explanation: Treasury stock (cost method) is debited at the price paid to reacquire the shares and shown as a contra-equity account, reducing total stockholders' equity. Companies never recognize gains or losses on transactions in their own stock through the income statement.

questions.stem: A company declares a 10% stock dividend (a 'small' stock dividend). The amount transferred from retained earnings is based on:
choices: Par value of the shares issued
Fair value of the shares issued at declaration
Original issue price
Nothing — only a memo entry is made
questions.explanation: A small stock dividend (generally under 20-25%) is capitalized at the fair value of the shares on the declaration date. A large stock dividend is recorded at par. A stock SPLIT changes par per share with no journal entry — only a memo.

questions.stem: Cumulative preferred stock dividends that were not declared in prior years are:
choices: Lost permanently once the year of nondeclaration has passed
Disclosed as dividends in arrears, not a liability until declared
Recognized as an expense in the period they would normally be paid
Recorded as a liability on the balance sheet at the end of each undeclared year
questions.explanation: Undeclared cumulative preferred dividends accumulate and must be paid before common dividends, but they are not a liability until the board declares them. They are disclosed in the notes as dividends in arrears.

questions.stem: Basic earnings per share is computed as:
choices: Net income divided by the entity's total assets at year end
(Net income plus interest) divided by diluted shares outstanding
(Net income − preferred dividends) divided by the weighted-average common shares outstanding
Net income divided by the number of common shares outstanding at the end of the reporting period
questions.explanation: Basic EPS = (net income available to common, i.e., net income minus preferred dividends) ÷ weighted-average common shares outstanding. Using a weighted average reflects shares actually outstanding during the period.

questions.stem: In computing diluted EPS, outstanding stock options are reflected using the:
choices: If-converted method
Treasury stock method
Effective-interest method
Equity method
questions.explanation: Dilutive options/warrants use the treasury stock method: assume exercise at the strike price and assume the proceeds buy back shares at the average market price; the net new shares increase the denominator. Convertible securities, by contrast, use the if-converted method.

questions.stem: A potentially convertible security is excluded from diluted EPS when its inclusion would:
choices: Decrease EPS (be dilutive)
Increase EPS (be antidilutive)
Leave EPS unchanged
Require a footnote
questions.explanation: Antidilutive securities — those whose inclusion would increase EPS (or decrease a loss per share) — are excluded from diluted EPS. Diluted EPS reflects only the dilutive potential common shares, never antidilutive ones.

questions.stem: Which transaction is reported in the statement of changes in stockholders' equity but does NOT affect net income?
choices: Sales revenue
Declaration of a cash dividend
Cost of goods sold
Depreciation expense
questions.explanation: Declaring a dividend is a distribution to owners: it reduces retained earnings and appears in the statement of changes in equity, but it is not an expense and never affects net income. Revenue, COGS, and depreciation all flow through income.

questions.stem: A temporary difference that will result in taxable amounts in future years (e.g., tax depreciation exceeding book depreciation now) gives rise to a:
choices: Deferred tax asset
Deferred tax liability
Permanent difference
Valuation allowance
questions.explanation: When a temporary difference will increase taxable income in the future (book basis exceeds tax basis of an asset), it creates a deferred tax liability — taxes deferred now that will reverse and be paid later. Differences that produce future deductions create deferred tax assets.

questions.stem: Which item is a PERMANENT difference (never reverses) between book and taxable income?
choices: Depreciation timing differences
Interest received on municipal (tax-exempt) bonds
Warranty expense accrued before paid
Unearned revenue taxed when received
questions.explanation: Tax-exempt municipal bond interest is recognized for book purposes but never taxed, so it is a permanent difference and creates no deferred tax. The others are temporary differences that reverse over time and do generate deferred taxes.

questions.stem: A valuation allowance against a deferred tax asset is recorded when:
choices: It is more likely than not (more than 50%) that some portion will NOT be realized
The enacted statutory tax rate changes during the reporting period
The company reports any net operating loss for the year
The deferred tax asset is virtually certain to be realized in full in future periods
questions.explanation: A deferred tax asset is reduced by a valuation allowance when it is more likely than not (a probability greater than 50%) that some or all of the DTA will not be realized, based on available evidence such as future taxable income. It is not automatic on every NOL.

questions.stem: Deferred tax assets and liabilities are measured using:
choices: The current year's average effective tax rate computed from the rate reconciliation
The tax rate enacted and expected to apply when the temporary difference reverses
The statutory rate that was in effect when the related asset was acquired
A flat twenty-one percent, regardless of when the difference reverses
questions.explanation: Deferred taxes are measured at the enacted tax rate expected to apply in the period the temporary difference reverses. When a new rate is enacted, deferred balances are remeasured and the effect runs through income from continuing operations.

questions.stem: Total income tax expense reported on the income statement equals:
choices: The cash taxes actually paid to the authorities during the year
Only the change in the deferred tax accounts during the year
The current tax expense (payable) plus the change in deferred tax balances
Only the current taxes payable for the year, with deferred amounts disclosed separately in the notes
questions.explanation: Income tax expense = current portion (taxes currently payable per the return) + deferred portion (the net change in deferred tax assets and liabilities). It is an accrual figure, not the cash actually paid.

questions.stem: On which income statement format does gross profit appear as a separate subtotal?
choices: A single-step income statement
Both formats, in the same position
The statement of cash flows
A multi-step income statement
questions.explanation: A multi-step income statement presents gross profit (net sales minus cost of goods sold) as a subtotal before operating expenses. A single-step statement groups all revenues and all expenses with no gross profit line.

questions.stem: Comprehensive income for a period is best described as the change in equity from:
choices: Revenues minus expenses from continuing operations only
Net income excluding the results of discontinued operations
Nonowner sources, equal to net income plus other comprehensive income
Net income plus dividends declared during the period
questions.explanation: Comprehensive income is the change in equity during a period from transactions and events with nonowners; it equals net income plus other comprehensive income. Owner transactions such as dividends and stock issuances are excluded.

questions.stem: Under U.S. GAAP, which item is reported in other comprehensive income rather than in net income?
choices: An unrealized holding gain on a trading debt security
An unrealized holding gain on an equity security with a readily determinable fair value
A realized gain on the sale of equipment
An unrealized holding gain on an available-for-sale debt security
questions.explanation: Unrealized holding gains and losses on available-for-sale debt securities are recognized in OCI. Trading securities and, since ASU 2016-01, equity securities with readily determinable fair values are remeasured through net income.

questions.stem: Net income is $400,000. During the year there was a $30,000 unrealized gain on available-for-sale debt securities, a $12,000 foreign currency translation loss, a $5,000 unrealized loss on the effective portion of a cash flow hedge, and $50,000 of dividends declared. Comprehensive income is:
questions.explanation: Comprehensive income = net income + net OCI. Net OCI = 30,000 - 12,000 - 5,000 = 13,000, so 400,000 + 13,000 = 413,000. Dividends declared are a transaction with owners and are excluded.

questions.stem: When an available-for-sale debt security whose gain was previously recognized in OCI is sold at that gain, the effect on other comprehensive income in the period of sale is:
choices: An increase in OCI equal to the realized gain
A direct credit to retained earnings
No effect, because the gain was already recognized in a prior period
A reclassification adjustment that decreases OCI so the gain is not counted twice
questions.explanation: The previously unrealized gain held in accumulated OCI is reclassified out of OCI and into net income when realized. The reclassification adjustment prevents the same gain from being included in comprehensive income twice.

questions.stem: U.S. GAAP allows an entity to present comprehensive income:
choices: Only within the statement of changes in stockholders' equity
In a single continuous statement, or in two consecutive statements
Only in the notes to the financial statements
Only as a single combined statement of income and comprehensive income
questions.explanation: Comprehensive income may be shown either in one continuous statement of comprehensive income or in two consecutive statements (an income statement followed by a statement of comprehensive income). Presenting it solely within the equity statement is not permitted.

questions.stem: Which item is presented, net of tax, in a separate section below income from continuing operations?
choices: The results of discontinued operations
A loss caused by a labor strike
A write-down of inventory to net realizable value
The effect of a change in an accounting estimate
questions.explanation: Discontinued operations are reported separately, net of tax, below income from continuing operations. The other items remain within continuing operations; separate extraordinary-item treatment was eliminated by ASU 2015-01.

questions.stem: Net sales were $900,000, cost of goods sold $540,000, selling and administrative expenses $180,000, interest expense $20,000, and there was a $15,000 gain on the sale of equipment. Operating income is:
questions.explanation: Operating income = gross profit - operating expenses = (900,000 - 540,000) - 180,000 = 180,000. Interest expense and the gain on sale of equipment are nonoperating and excluded from operating income.

questions.stem: Accumulated other comprehensive income is reported as:
choices: A line item within the determination of net income
A liability remeasured to fair value each period
A required line within the statement of cash flows
A separate component of stockholders' equity on the balance sheet
questions.explanation: Items of OCI accumulate in accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI), a separate component of equity on the balance sheet. Comprehensive income is the flow for the period; AOCI is the cumulative balance.

questions.stem: In a single-step income statement, income before taxes is computed as:
choices: Gross profit minus operating expenses
Net sales minus cost of goods sold
Total revenues and gains minus total expenses and losses
Operating income plus other comprehensive income
questions.explanation: A single-step statement totals all revenues and gains, totals all expenses and losses, and subtracts in a single step. There are no intermediate subtotals such as gross profit or operating income.

questions.stem: Which of the following is recognized in other comprehensive income?
choices: Amortization of prior service cost into net periodic pension cost
Interest cost on the projected benefit obligation
Prior service cost arising during the current period in a defined benefit pension plan
The service cost component of net periodic pension cost
questions.explanation: Prior service cost (and net actuarial gains and losses) arising during the period are recognized in OCI and later amortized into net periodic pension cost. Service cost, interest cost, and the amortization itself are components of pension expense in net income.

questions.stem: A customer that was already in financial difficulty at the balance sheet date settled its receivable for a reduced amount before the statements were issued. This is:
choices: An event that is neither recognized nor disclosed
A change in accounting estimate applied prospectively
A recognized subsequent event requiring adjustment of the financial statements
A nonrecognized subsequent event requiring only note disclosure
questions.explanation: A recognized (Type I) subsequent event provides more evidence about a condition that existed at the balance sheet date - here the customer's difficulty existed at year end - so the statements are adjusted. Conditions arising only after year end are nonrecognized (Type II) and merely disclosed.

questions.stem: After the balance sheet date but before issuance, a fire destroyed one of the company's major plants. This event should be:
choices: Disclosed in the notes as a nonrecognized subsequent event
Recorded as a loss in the current-year income statement
Reported as a prior period adjustment to retained earnings
Ignored until the following reporting period
questions.explanation: The fire reflects a condition that arose after the balance sheet date, so it is a nonrecognized (Type II) subsequent event. The statements are not adjusted, but a material event like this is disclosed so the statements are not misleading.

questions.stem: The summary of significant accounting policies note would most likely disclose:
choices: The detailed components of the income tax provision
The maturity dates of the company's long-term debt
The detailed composition of the plant assets balance
The depreciation method used for property, plant, and equipment
questions.explanation: The significant accounting policies note describes the methods chosen, such as depreciation method, inventory costing, and revenue recognition. Specific balances and their composition are disclosed in other notes, not the policies note.

questions.stem: Disclosure of related party transactions must include all of the following EXCEPT:
choices: The nature of the relationship between the related parties that entered into the transactions
A description of the transactions, including their terms and manner of settlement
A representation that the transactions were on arm's-length terms, absent substantiation
The dollar amounts of the transactions for each period presented
questions.explanation: Related party disclosures cover the nature of the relationship, a description of the transactions, and the dollar amounts. An entity must not assert that terms were equivalent to arm's-length transactions unless that representation can be substantiated.

questions.stem: An unadjusted quoted price for an identical asset in an active market is categorized within the fair value hierarchy as:
choices: Level 2
Outside the fair value hierarchy
Level 3
Level 1
